_comment_alert_policy_names: Slug-derived keys for policy names. Slug = lowercased name with non-alphanumerics → underscore. If a policy is added in DB without a corresponding key here, the live English name from the DB is used as graceful fallback (slugify('Account lockouts') → 'account_lockouts').

alert_policy_names.ual_mail_flow_rule_disabled_or_removed: Mail flow rule disabled or removed

_comment_alert_message_prefix: Custom alert message prefixes that don't match the parent alert_policy_names. CA + Intune drift alerts use these literal English prefixes server-side; the renderAlertMessage helper detects them at display time and substitutes the translated form.
_comment_alert_policy_descriptions: Same slug-keyed pattern as alert_policy_names. Drop more rows here as new alert policies ship in the DB.

alert_policy_descriptions.known_good_app_drift: An application previously marked known-good gained one or more permissions beyond its approved baseline. Removing permissions does not fire; only growth past the baseline does. Source: Microsoft Graph, compared on Refresh and on the daily known-good loop.
alert_policy_descriptions.sign_ins_blocked_by_conditional_access: Tenant-wide telemetry: daily count of sign-ins blocked by Conditional Access policies. Feeds the Daily Activity donut chart.
alert_policy_descriptions.impossible_travel_detections: Risk detections for unlikely travel patterns
alert_policy_descriptions.account_lockouts: Account lockouts due to incorrect sign-in attempts
alert_policy_descriptions.user_login_failure_summary: Tenant-wide telemetry: daily count of user login failures. Feeds the Daily Activity donut chart.
alert_policy_descriptions.admin_blocked_by_conditional_access: A user in a privileged administrative role was blocked by a Conditional Access policy. Admin logins should succeed; any CA block of an admin UPN is treated as critical.
alert_policy_descriptions.malware_after_delivery: Malware campaign detected after email delivery
alert_policy_descriptions.malware_in_sharepoint_onedrive: Malware detected in SharePoint or OneDrive files
alert_policy_descriptions.external_forwarding_rule_creation: Creation of email forwarding rule to external address — detected via snapshot comparison of inbox rules
alert_policy_descriptions.global_admin_privilege_elevation: User elevated to Global Administrator role
alert_policy_descriptions.unusual_file_deletion_volume: Unusual volume of file deletions detected
alert_policy_descriptions.inbound_malware_blocked: Inbound email containing malware was blocked by Exchange Online Protection
alert_policy_descriptions.inbound_phishing_blocked: Inbound phishing email was blocked or junked by Exchange Online Protection
alert_policy_descriptions.inbound_spam_blocked: Inbound spam email was blocked or junked by Exchange Online Protection
alert_policy_descriptions.email_threat_volume_spike: Unusual volume of blocked email threats — possible targeted campaign
alert_policy_descriptions.foreign_login_non_compliant_device: Successful sign-in from outside allowed countries on a non-compliant or unmanaged device
alert_policy_descriptions.oauth_consent_grant: User or admin granted OAuth permissions to an application — potential consent phishing attack
alert_policy_descriptions.inbox_rule_created: New inbox rule created — attackers use rules to auto-forward or hide emails
alert_policy_descriptions.inbox_rule_deleted: Inbox rule was deleted or disabled — may indicate cleanup of malicious rules or covering tracks. Dashboard-only (no email) by default; flip notification_target to enable alerts.
alert_policy_descriptions.suspicious_risk_detection: Medium or high risk detection from Entra ID Protection (anomalous token, unfamiliar sign-in properties, etc.)
alert_policy_descriptions.admin_role_assignment: User assigned to a privileged administrative role (including Global Administrator)
alert_policy_descriptions.conditional_access_policy_changed: A Conditional Access policy was created, modified, or deleted — critical security configuration change
alert_policy_descriptions.mailbox_level_forwarding_enabled: A user mailbox now has Set-Mailbox -ForwardingSmtpAddress set. Detected via snapshot-delta on Get-Mailbox output. Sibling vector to inbox-rule forwarding — covers the case where forwarding is configured directly on the mailbox rather than via an inbox rule. Source: PowerShell (Graph does not expose this property).
alert_policy_descriptions.ca_policy_drift_detected: A conditional access policy has drifted from its expected template configuration.
alert_policy_descriptions.ca_policy_drift_remediated: A drifted conditional access policy was automatically remediated back to template.
alert_policy_descriptions.ca_exemption_list_changed: A CA policy exemption list (excludeUsers/excludeGroups) was modified outside Panoptica365. Informational — no security impact unless paired with other policy drift.
alert_policy_descriptions.intune_policy_drift_detected: An Intune policy has drifted from its expected template configuration.
alert_policy_descriptions.panoptica_system_health: Internal system failures (summary delivery, SMTP errors, etc.)
alert_policy_descriptions.security_setting_drift_detected: A security setting under Panoptica365 baseline has drifted from its applied/matched value. Fired when the slow-tier poll detects current state no longer matches the captured baseline. Severity is computed per-alert from the setting's priority (critical→severe, high→high, etc.) and overrides the default below.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_mailbox_permission_added: A mailbox permission was granted to a user other than the mailbox owner. Carries operator UPN, grantee, access rights, and IP.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_anomalous_geo_file_access: A user accessed or downloaded a SharePoint or OneDrive file from a country outside the tenant's CA-derived allowed list. Suppressed when the underlying sign-in went through a compliant-device CA gate.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_oauth_consent_app_role_grant: A user or admin consented to an application's permissions. Severity escalates when high-risk scopes (Mail.*, Files.Read.All, offline_access, full_access_as_app, Directory.*.All) are requested or when admin-consented.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_service_principal_credentials_added: A new client secret or certificate was added to a service principal or application. Persistence indicator — once an attacker has a credential on an existing SP, they retain access through password rotations.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_privileged_role_assignment: A user was assigned a privileged Entra role. Severity computed per-role: severe for Global Admin / Privileged Role Admin / Privileged Auth Admin / Security Admin; high for Application / Cloud App / Exchange / SharePoint / Teams admins.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_transport_rule_changed: A tenant-wide transport rule was created, modified, removed, enabled, or disabled. Severe when the rule has BlindCopyTo or RedirectMessageTo (mail-exfiltration vectors).
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_mailbox_forwarding_configured_ual: Set-Mailbox enabled ForwardingSmtpAddress or ForwardingAddress on a mailbox. UAL-enriched: carries operator UPN and IP. Severe for external-domain forwarding.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_inbox_rule_changed_ual_enriched: New-InboxRule, Set-InboxRule, or Disable-InboxRule fired. Severe when operator UPN ≠ mailbox owner or rule has Forward/Redirect/Delete; high for evasion-folder MoveToFolder patterns.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_anonymous_link_created: A SharePoint or OneDrive resource was shared via an anonymous (anyone-with-the-link) sharing link. Top SMB exfiltration vector.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_mass_file_deletion_burst: A user deleted more than the threshold (default 50) SharePoint / OneDrive files in a 15-minute window. Ransomware or insider-sabotage indicator. Anti-flapping cooldown: 60 minutes per user.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_ediscovery_compliance_search: A Purview Compliance / Mailbox eDiscovery search was created. Auto-attributed to MSP audit log when matched within ±15 minutes (legitimate Purview activity); unattributed events surface as alerts.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_mail_flow_rule_disabled_or_removed: A transport rule was disabled or removed (Disable-TransportRule, Remove-TransportRule, Set-TransportRule State=Disabled). Sabotage pattern — operator silently turning off EOP / anti-spam protections. Panoptica365-managed rules excluded.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_application_uri_redirecturi_modified: An application registration had its ReplyUrls, IdentifierUris, or Web.RedirectUris modified. Token-theft setup pattern. Cosmetic property changes (display name, owner) are filtered out.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_send_as_sendonbehalf_permission_gran: A SendAs or SendOnBehalf permission was granted on a mailbox (Add-RecipientPermission with SendAs, or Set-Mailbox with GrantSendOnBehalfTo). Impersonation setup.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_microsoft_defender_alert: A Microsoft Defender XDR alert (Defender for Endpoint, O365, Identity, or Cloud Apps) surfaced via UAL. Severity inherited from Microsoft (Informational/Low/Medium/High). IncidentId captured for incident-correlation.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_site_collection_administrator_added: A user was elevated to Site Collection Administrator on a SharePoint site. Privilege escalation that bypasses tenant-role detection.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_outbound_connector_changed: A tenant outbound connector was created, modified, or removed. Different layer than transport rules — connectors operate at the mail-flow infrastructure layer. SMTP exfiltration setup.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_mailbox_disabled_or_removed: A mailbox was disabled (Disable-Mailbox) or removed (Remove-Mailbox). Data destruction pattern. Auto-attributed to MSP audit log when matched within ±15 minutes.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_org_wide_exchange_config_tampered: A Set-OrganizationConfig cmdlet modified one or more security-relevant org-wide properties (AutoForwardingEnabled, ModernAuthEnabled, OAuth2ClientProfileEnabled, AdminAuditLogEnabled, etc.). Severe on apex tampers.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_mfa_method_tampered_admin_on_behalf_: An admin registered or removed an MFA / security-info method on behalf of another user. Severe on removal (account-takeover finishing move). Self-changes (operator==target) are suppressed. Auto-attributed to MSP audit log.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_exchange_role_group_membership_chang: A change to Exchange RBAC role group membership. Separate from Entra roles. Severe when Organization Management or Discovery Management is touched.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_per_mailbox_audit_tampered: A Set-Mailbox cmdlet flipped AuditEnabled to False or AuditBypassEnabled to True on a specific mailbox. Counter-EXO-09 at per-mailbox level — turn off the camera before the heist.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_admin_initiated_password_reset: An admin reset another user's password (operator ≠ target). Auto-attributed to MSP audit log when matched within ±15 minutes (legitimate helpdesk activity).
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_legacy_protocol_re_enabled_per_mailb: A Set-CASMailbox cmdlet re-enabled a legacy protocol (IMAP, POP, ActiveSync, EWS, MAPI) on a specific mailbox. MFA-bypass vector. Severe when 2+ protocols re-enabled simultaneously.
alert_policy_descriptions.ual_microsoft_defender_incident: A Microsoft Defender XDR incident — Microsoft's correlation of multiple Defender alerts into a single multi-stage attack story. Different layer than Bundle D-1 Defender alerts (single events). Source: Microsoft Graph Security API (/v1.0/security/incidents). Fires on (a) new incident arrival, (b) severity escalation on existing incident, (c) new alerts joined to existing incident. License-gated to tenants with Defender XDR (Defender for Business + Defender for O365 P1 minimum).

_comment_alert_explanations: Plain-English educational content for each alert policy, surfaced via the ⓘ icon on the Alert Policies admin page and on the alert detail slideout. Audience is L1 MSP technicians who need to understand WHY the alert exists and WHAT to do when it fires. Five fixed sections per policy: what_is_this, why_it_matters, attack_vectors, what_to_do, example_scenario. Slug pattern matches alert_policy_names. No internal IDs, no error codes, no PowerShell cmdlets in copy. Newlines (\n\n) separate paragraphs; bullets allowed via leading dashes per line.

known_good_app_drift.what_is_this:
You marked this application “known good” after reviewing the permissions it held at the time, and Panoptica saved that exact permission set as a baseline. This alert means the app has since gained permissions beyond that baseline — it now holds access you did not approve.

This is the Accept-Drift model applied to OAuth apps: marking an app known-good is not “ignore forever,” it is “this exact set is fine — tell me if it grows.”
known_good_app_drift.why_it_matters:
An app you already approved is a trusted part of the tenant. Attackers know a quiet permission escalation on an existing, trusted app is far less likely to be noticed than a brand-new consent. Adding Mail.ReadWrite or Directory.ReadWrite.All to an app nobody questions is a clean persistence and data-access path that survives password resets and MFA changes — the same fundamentals as consent phishing (see the OAuth Consent Phishing lesson in the Learn hub), hidden inside something already on your known-good list.

Removing permissions is never flagged — only growth past the baseline is, because growth is the direction that adds risk.
known_good_app_drift.attack_vectors:
What to look at when this fires:

- New high-impact scopes. Mail.*, Files.*.All, Directory.ReadWrite.All, full_access_as_app, or AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All added to an app that didn’t have them is the headline signal.

- New application (app-only) permissions. App-only grants act without a signed-in user and are the strongest persistence — weight them above delegated additions.

- New credentials on an app registration. A fresh client secret or certificate on a high-permission app is how an attacker takes over an app you already trust.

- New redirect URIs. An added reply URL on an app registration is token-theft setup.

- Legitimate change. The vendor expanded the product, or an admin deliberately granted more. Confirm out-of-band, then re-save to set the new baseline.
known_good_app_drift.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the app’s row in the Applications tab and expand it to see the full current permission set; the alert lists exactly what was added.

Step 2. Decide whether the addition is expected. Check the Change Log and ask the operator/admin whether they granted it. A verified publisher expanding scopes is usually benign; an unverified or generic-looking app gaining Mail/Directory access is not.

Step 3. If unexpected, treat it as a live consent-abuse incident. In Entra → Enterprise Applications (or App registrations), review the new grant/credential, remove it, revoke the app’s tokens, and audit what it accessed since the change.

Step 4. If expected, re-open the app in the Applications tab and Save it again to capture the new permission set as the baseline. The drift clears and the app returns to known-good.

Panoptica takes no action on the tenant — every change is made by you in Entra.
known_good_app_drift.example_scenario:
Six weeks ago you reviewed “Contoso Backup” — a verified-publisher backup tool with Files.Read.All and Mail.Read — and marked it known good. This morning the daily loop finds it now also holds Mail.ReadWrite and Directory.ReadWrite.All, added at 02:40 via an admin-consent grant.

That is drift in the dangerous direction. A backup tool has no need for directory write. Either the vendor shipped an over-broad update, or an attacker who compromised an admin escalated an app already on your trusted list precisely because it would not draw a second look.

You’d expand the row to confirm the added scopes, check the Change Log for a matching operator action, and if none, treat the admin as compromised — pull the new grant in Entra, revoke the app’s tokens, audit mailbox and directory access since 02:40, and reset the admin. If the vendor legitimately expanded the product and the customer approved it, re-Save the app to set the new baseline so it stops alerting.

alert_explanations.section_what_to_do: What to do when it fires
alert_explanations.no_content_yet: A detailed explanation for this alert hasn't been written yet. Check back after the next Panoptica365 update.

microsoft_planned_change.what_is_this:
Microsoft just announced — in the Message Center, the admin-center feed where Microsoft posts every roadmap change — that it's about to change how something in Microsoft 365 works. Panoptica365 reads that feed once a day from one tenant you chose, hands each new post to AI, and fires this alert only when the change looks like it touches a setting we monitor for you.

This is not an attack and not drift someone caused on purpose. It's Microsoft moving the floor under your feet. The alert names the change, when it lands, and which of your tenants it may affect.
microsoft_planned_change.why_it_matters:
You spend real effort locking tenants down — auditing, mailbox forwarding, Safe Links, MFA posture. Then Microsoft flips a default, retires a control, or changes who a policy applies to, and a setting you thought was nailed shut quietly drifts. Nobody touched it. It just stopped meaning what it meant last week.

That's the third kind of drift. You already watch for the drift an operator causes and the drift an attacker causes. This is the drift Microsoft causes — and it's the easiest to miss, because there's no login to investigate and no config change in your audit log. The only signal is the announcement itself, usually with weeks of lead time. Catch it now and you adjust on your schedule. Miss it and you find out when something breaks or a control silently weakens.
microsoft_planned_change.attack_vectors:
This alert isn't about an attacker — it's about why a Microsoft change can hurt you even when no one is acting maliciously.

- A hardened default gets softened. Microsoft loosens a default you were relying on, and tenants that inherited the strict behavior quietly fall back to the looser one.

- A control changes scope. A policy that used to apply to everyone starts applying only to licensed users, or excludes a group it used to include — so coverage shrinks without any setting visibly changing.

- A feature is retired or replaced. The control you configured is deprecated in favor of a new surface; your old setting stops being enforced and the new one isn't turned on yet.

- A change is tier-gated. The announcement only bites at a higher license tier (P2/E5). Your Business Premium tenants may be untouched — but you want to confirm that, not assume it.

The through-line: a setting you believe is locked down can weaken on its own. This alert is your early warning.
microsoft_planned_change.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the alert and read what Microsoft is changing and the date it lands. Use the "Read the Message Center post" link to see Microsoft's original wording — it carries the detail and any opt-out window.

Step 2. Check which monitored area the AI flagged and confirm the assumed license tier. If the change only matters at P2/E5 and your tenants are Business Premium, note it and move on. If it bites at Business Premium, keep going.

Step 3. Look at the affected-tenants list on the alert. v1 lists every enabled tenant — Microsoft defaults touch everyone — so treat it as "check these," not "all of these are broken."

Step 4. Decide your move before the date lands: re-apply or tighten the relevant template, push the setting, or accept the change deliberately. Doing it now, on your timeline, beats reacting after it ships.

Step 5. After the change date, re-poll the affected tenants and confirm the monitored setting still reads the way you expect. If Microsoft's change moved it, you'll see it in the security posture and can remediate.
microsoft_planned_change.example_scenario:
On a Monday, Panoptica365's daily Message Center pull picks up a post: Microsoft is changing the default behavior of external email forwarding controls in Exchange Online, effective in about three weeks. The AI matches it to the mailbox-forwarding control you monitor and fires one MSP-wide alert listing your 12 managed tenants — not 12 separate alerts.

You'd ignored Message Center for months because it's mostly noise. But here's how this one would have slipped past without the alert: the change doesn't show up in any sign-in log or audit trail. Three weeks later the default quietly shifts, and on a handful of tenants that relied on the old default, external forwarding becomes possible again. You don't find out until a user forwards a mailbox to a personal Gmail and it just works — the exact thing you'd configured the tenant to block.

Because the alert fired with three weeks of lead time, you instead open it that Monday, read Microsoft's post, confirm it hits Business Premium, and push your forwarding-block template to all 12 tenants before the change lands. The floor moved; you'd already nailed it back down.

account_lockouts.what_is_this:
Microsoft Entra ID has temporarily locked one or more user accounts because of too many failed sign-in attempts in a short window. Entra's built-in Smart Lockout feature does the locking automatically; Panoptica365 is reporting that it happened so you can investigate why.

The lockout itself is short (usually around a minute on first lockout, longer on repeats). The account unlocks on its own — you don't need to do anything to release it.
account_lockouts.why_it_matters:
Account lockouts are rarely innocent in modern environments. Password managers, browser auto-fill, and saved credentials on phones mean a real user almost never types their password wrong enough times in a row to trigger Entra's lockout threshold.

When a lockout fires, the most likely explanation is that someone is actively trying to guess the user's password — either an outside attacker running an automated brute-force or password-spray attack, or a stale device somewhere still using an outdated password. The lockout is a defensive win (Entra blocked the attempt), but it tells you the account is being targeted, and that's a signal to investigate, not dismiss.
account_lockouts.attack_vectors:
Three patterns typically produce account lockouts:

- Password spray. An attacker tries one common password (for example "Spring2026!") against many users in the tenant at once. Each user has a separate failure counter, so a few unlucky users hit lockout while most show only a handful of failures. Often comes from a small number of foreign IP addresses hitting many users in the same hour.

- Targeted brute force. An attacker focuses on a single high-value account — an admin, a finance user, or an executive — and tries many passwords against it from one or more sources. The same user shows dozens or hundreds of failures over a short window.

- Stale credentials on a forgotten device. A user's old phone, an unused app, a printer, or a saved Wi-Fi profile is still trying to authenticate with an outdated password. Looks identical to a real attack at first glance until you check the source IP — it'll usually be a single residential or office IP that the user actually uses.

The first two are real threats. The third is noise but should still be tracked down so the alert doesn't keep firing.
account_lockouts.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open Microsoft Entra admin center → Sign-in logs and filter to the affected user over the past 24 hours. Look at the failure events, not just the lockout itself.

Step 2. Check the source IPs and locations. Many failures from one IP in a country the user actually works in usually means a stale device. Many failures from many IPs across multiple countries means a credential attack.

Step 3. Contact the user. Ask whether they've recently had password trouble, set up a new device, or have any old phones, tablets, or apps that might still be using their previous password.

Step 4. If the source looks suspicious — unfamiliar countries, anonymizing IPs (VPN/Tor), or a burst from many IPs in a short window — treat it as a credential attack. Reset the user's password, revoke their active sessions in Entra, and confirm MFA is enrolled and being enforced for that account.

Step 5. If you see the same lockout pattern hitting multiple users in the tenant in the same time window, it's a password spray. Escalate, confirm MFA is enforced for everyone (not just the locked-out users), and review sign-in logs across the tenant for any successful sign-ins from the same IP range — those are the accounts that got compromised.
account_lockouts.example_scenario:
On a Tuesday morning, three of your customer's users get locked out within 20 minutes of each other. None of them were on vacation or traveling. You open the Entra sign-in logs and filter to those three users for the past hour.

All three show the same pattern: 12 to 18 failed sign-ins each, all from a handful of IP addresses in a country none of those users have ever signed in from before. The username/password combination is failing with "invalid credentials," not with a Conditional Access block.

This is a password spray. The attacker is trying common passwords against a list of your customer's user accounts. Entra's Smart Lockout caught the three users whose accounts hit the failure threshold first. The risk now is any user whose password the attacker actually guessed correctly — those sign-ins succeeded silently and won't appear as lockouts.

You'd: reset the three locked-out users, force-revoke all their sessions, search the sign-in logs for any successful sign-ins from that IP range over the same window (those are likely compromises), confirm MFA is enforced tenant-wide, and consider blocking that source country in Conditional Access if the customer has no legitimate business there.

sign_ins_blocked_by_conditional_access.what_is_this: This isn't a per-event alert — it's tenant telemetry. Panoptica365 counts how many sign-ins the customer's Conditional Access policies blocked over the day and feeds that count into the Daily Activity donut chart on the tenant dashboard. No notification fires for a single block; only the running trend matters.
sign_ins_blocked_by_conditional_access.why_it_matters: Watching the trend tells you two things. First, that the customer's CA policies are actually doing something — a tenant whose count is consistently zero either has no protective policies in place or has scoped them to nobody useful. Second, that something unusual is happening when the count spikes. A normal tenant has a low, fairly steady number of CA blocks per day. A sudden multi-fold jump usually means an attacker is hitting the tenant from a blocked source and getting bounced.
sign_ins_blocked_by_conditional_access.attack_vectors:
On its own, a high block count is a defensive success, not an attack signal. The signal you care about is change.

- A credential attack from a blocked country or unmanaged device pool. The attacker doesn't know they're being blocked and keeps trying.
- A new CA policy was just rolled out and is catching legitimate user traffic that needs adjustment.
- A user changed location (travel, new home office) and is bumping into a country-restricted policy.
- A misconfigured app or device is repeatedly retrying with broken authentication.
sign_ins_blocked_by_conditional_access.what_to_do:
Step 1. Look at the Daily Activity chart's CA-blocks slice over the past 7 to 30 days. A flat baseline with small bumps is healthy.

Step 2. If you see a sharp spike, open the customer's Entra sign-in logs and filter to "Failure → Blocked by Conditional Access" for that window.

Step 3. If the blocks cluster on a small number of users from unfamiliar IPs or countries, treat the affected accounts as potential credential-attack targets — reset, revoke, confirm MFA.

Step 4. If the blocks are spread across many users from normal IPs, suspect a CA policy misconfiguration — review what changed in CA recently and walk it back if needed.
sign_ins_blocked_by_conditional_access.example_scenario:
A customer's CA-blocks count has hovered between 5 and 15 per day for months. One morning the chart shows 280 blocks in four hours. You open Entra sign-in logs and filter to that window: 280 failures across 230 different user accounts, all coming from two countries the customer has never operated from, all blocked by the same geo-restriction CA policy.

This is a password spray against the customer's full user list. The attacker tried each account once or twice and was bounced by the geo block on every attempt. CA did its job perfectly — the chart spike is how you knew it was happening. You'd review whether any users from those two countries had legitimate sign-ins (rare), and confirm MFA enforcement across the tenant in case a non-blocked-country attempt slipped through.

impossible_travel_detections.what_is_this: Microsoft Entra ID Protection has flagged a sign-in pattern that would require physically impossible travel — for example, a user signing in from Toronto and then from Lagos within 90 minutes. Entra computes this by comparing successful sign-in locations against the time between them; if the implied travel speed exceeds what's plausible (jet airline + ground transit), it raises an Impossible Travel risk detection.
impossible_travel_detections.why_it_matters: Impossible travel is one of the strongest single-event indicators that an account has been compromised. A real human can't be in two places at once, so seeing both sign-ins succeed means either (a) the user's credentials are being used by someone else from a different location, (b) the user is on a VPN or proxy that's masking their real location, or (c) the user is using a service that signs in on their behalf from a cloud datacenter. Cases (b) and (c) are noise but need to be confirmed; case (a) is account takeover.
impossible_travel_detections.attack_vectors:
Three scenarios produce impossible travel detections:

- Account compromise. The attacker has the user's password (and possibly bypassed MFA via token theft, AiTM phishing, or session-cookie theft) and is signing in from their own location while the legitimate user is also active.

- VPN or anonymizer use. The user enabled a VPN exiting from another country between two sign-ins. Common with consumer VPNs and corporate VPNs that exit through a hub region.

- Cloud service signing in on the user's behalf. Some integrations (mobile apps, mail clients on managed devices) authenticate from datacenters in regions far from the user. Usually consistent over time — same datacenter every day.
impossible_travel_detections.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the Entra sign-in logs for that user covering the impossible travel window. Note the two locations, the IPs, the apps used, and the device/client info.

Step 2. If one of the two sign-ins is from a known-corporate IP or a known datacenter the user normally hits, you're likely looking at a VPN or service-on-behalf — confirm with the user.

Step 3. If both sign-ins are from residential or unfamiliar IPs in countries that don't match the user's profile, treat this as account takeover. Reset the user's password, revoke all active sessions, force MFA re-registration, and review what the impostor session accessed (mail, files, admin actions).

Step 4. Check whether the user has any inbox rules, mail forwarding, or token-grants created in the past 24 hours. Attackers move fast to establish persistence after a successful compromise.
impossible_travel_detections.example_scenario:
On a Wednesday at 14:02, a user signs in from their normal home IP in Montreal. At 14:38, the same user appears to sign in from Vietnam. Entra raises an Impossible Travel detection.

You open the sign-in logs. The Montreal sign-in is from the user's usual residential IP, on their managed laptop, with MFA satisfied via Authenticator. The Vietnam sign-in is from a residential IP, on an unfamiliar device, MFA satisfied via SMS. The user has no travel scheduled and is not using a VPN.

This is account takeover via SMS-MFA fatigue or SIM-swap. The attacker has the password and intercepted or fatigued an SMS code. You'd reset the password, revoke all sessions immediately, remove the SMS method from the account in favor of Authenticator-only, check the inbox for newly-created rules (forwarding, deletion, hide), check OneDrive/SharePoint for downloads in the past hour, and notify the customer's security contact.

user_login_failure_summary.what_is_this: This isn't a per-event alert — it's tenant telemetry. Panoptica365 counts the total number of failed sign-in attempts across the customer's tenant over the day and feeds that count into the Daily Activity donut chart. Failures here include wrong passwords, blocked sign-ins, MFA failures, and account-locked attempts. No notification fires per failure; the trend is what tells the story.
user_login_failure_summary.why_it_matters: A small steady stream of failures is normal in any tenant — users mistype, devices retry with stale tokens, ex-employees' phones still ping. What matters is the trend over time and any sharp deviation. A flat baseline of 20 failures per day that suddenly jumps to 1,500 is the kind of thing you want to know about, even if no individual failure is suspicious enough to fire its own alert.
user_login_failure_summary.attack_vectors:
Sharp jumps in the failure count usually mean one of:

- Password spray against the tenant. An attacker tries one common password against many users. Each failure shows as "invalid credentials" with attempts spread across many accounts from a small number of source IPs.

- Brute force against a single user. Many failures concentrated on one or two accounts, often a high-value target like an admin or executive.

- A misconfigured app or device. A printer, conferencing room display, or service account is retrying constantly with bad credentials. Look for one source IP and one user account.

- A botched mass password change. IT pushed a password change to many users and old cached credentials on phones and apps are now failing in bulk. Usually self-resolves within a day.
user_login_failure_summary.what_to_do:
Step 1. Look at the Daily Activity chart's failure slice over the past 7 to 30 days. Establish what the customer's normal baseline looks like.

Step 2. On a spike, open Entra sign-in logs filtered to Failure status for that window. Look at how the failures distribute across users and IPs.

Step 3. Many users, few IPs → password spray. Confirm by checking whether any of those sign-ins eventually succeeded (those are the real compromises). Reset, revoke, confirm MFA.

Step 4. Few users, many or one IP → targeted brute force or stale-device noise. Contact the user, identify the source.

Step 5. Many users, many IPs, broad pattern → likely benign (mass password change, app update). Confirm with the customer's IT contact before chasing it as an incident.
user_login_failure_summary.example_scenario:
A customer's normal failure baseline is around 30 to 60 per day. On a Monday morning, the count is at 4,200 by 11 AM. You drill into Entra sign-in logs for the morning window.

The failures cluster on roughly 80 different user accounts, all from a single source IP in a foreign datacenter. Each user account has 50 to 100 failures with different password attempts. This is a credential-stuffing attack — the attacker has a list of email/password combinations from a third-party breach and is testing them against the customer's tenant.

You'd block the source IP at the CA level, run a search for any successful sign-ins from that IP over the same window (those are confirmed compromises), reset the affected accounts, force MFA re-registration if any accounts had MFA disabled, and review whether the customer is enrolled in Have-I-Been-Pwned-style breach monitoring.

admin_blocked_by_conditional_access.what_is_this: A user holding a privileged administrative role (Global Admin, Privileged Role Admin, Security Admin, Exchange Admin, etc.) was blocked by a Conditional Access policy when trying to sign in. Unlike regular CA blocks (which are background telemetry), an admin block is treated as critical — admins should always be able to sign in successfully when they need to.
admin_blocked_by_conditional_access.why_it_matters: If a CA policy is blocking an admin, one of three things is true: someone is trying to sign in as the admin from an unauthorized location or device, the admin's CA exclusion has gone missing, or the admin is in an unusual situation (travel, lost device) that needs handling. All three are worth a real-time alert. Admin compromise is one of the highest-impact outcomes possible in a Microsoft 365 tenant — once an attacker has Global Admin, they can do anything the legitimate admin can: exfiltrate mail, create persistence, disable other admins, exempt themselves from MFA.
admin_blocked_by_conditional_access.attack_vectors:
Common reasons an admin gets blocked:

- Credential attack on the admin account. The attacker has the admin's password and is trying to sign in from an unfamiliar country or device. CA blocks the attempt because the admin profile requires location/device compliance.

- Stolen/leaked admin credentials being tested. Same as above but the source pattern (unfamiliar IP, unusual user agent, anonymizer) tips it off.

- Recent CA policy change inadvertently affected the admin. Someone modified a policy without an admin-exclusion and the next admin sign-in trips it.

- Legitimate admin on travel or new device. The admin really is trying to sign in from somewhere new and the policy is doing its job.
admin_blocked_by_conditional_access.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify which admin and which CA policy. Open the alert and note the user, the policy name, and the failure reason.

Step 2. Verify with the admin out-of-band (phone, in person, separate channel — not email, which the attacker may control). Are they actively trying to sign in from a new location?

Step 3. If the admin confirms the attempt is theirs, work with them to either complete it via an approved path (compliant device, allowed country) or temporarily adjust the CA policy.

Step 4. If the admin denies the attempt, treat it as active credential attack. Reset the admin's password immediately, revoke all active sessions, force MFA re-registration, and review the past 24 hours of admin activity in the Entra audit log for any actions taken using the admin account.

Step 5. After resolution, review whether MFA is enforced for the admin via a phishing-resistant method (FIDO2, Authenticator number-matching) — SMS or voice MFA on an admin account is no longer acceptable.
admin_blocked_by_conditional_access.example_scenario:
At 03:14 on a Saturday, your customer's Global Admin account triggers a CA block — sign-in from an IP in Russia, blocked by the "admin allowed countries" policy. The admin lives in Calgary and is asleep.

This is a credential attack. The block was a defensive win — the attacker's sign-in attempt was rejected — but it tells you the attacker has the admin's password. You'd immediately: reset the admin's password (forcing the attacker out of any cached sessions), revoke all the admin's active sessions in Entra, confirm MFA is enrolled with a phishing-resistant method (replace SMS if present), pull the past 24 hours of admin sign-in attempts to see whether any prior attempts succeeded from the same IP range, and audit the Entra activity log for any actions the admin appeared to take in the past 24 hours that the real admin didn't perform.

foreign_login_non_compliant_device.what_is_this: A user successfully signed in from a country outside the customer's allowed list, on a device that's either non-compliant (failing Intune compliance policy) or not managed at all. Panoptica365 only fires this when the sign-in succeeded — meaning Conditional Access didn't block it. Successful foreign sign-ins on uncontrolled devices are the classic post-compromise pattern.
foreign_login_non_compliant_device.why_it_matters: Most modern CA policies require either an allowed country OR a compliant device. This alert fires when a sign-in slips through both gates: the country is unfamiliar AND the device isn't under management. That combination shouldn't happen in a hardened tenant. When it does, it usually means either the user is an exception (a contractor, an executive on travel with a personal device) or the account is being used by someone else.
foreign_login_non_compliant_device.attack_vectors:
The most likely scenarios:

- Account takeover where the attacker is operating from their own country on their own device. CA policies that depend on "allowed country OR compliant device" don't catch this if the policy was scoped too loosely.

- A legitimate user traveling internationally who's signing in from a personal phone or laptop because their managed device is at home. Common with executives.

- A contractor or external user given a regular account instead of a guest account.
foreign_login_non_compliant_device.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the user, the country, and the device fingerprint. Compare the country to the user's known travel patterns or work locations.

Step 2. If the user is plausibly traveling, contact them out-of-band to confirm the sign-in is theirs. Ask what device they used.

Step 3. If they confirm: work with the customer to formalize the exception — either enroll the device into Intune, switch the user to a managed device, or scope a temporary CA exception.

Step 4. If they deny: treat as account takeover. Reset password, revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration, and audit recent activity for the user (mail rules, OneDrive downloads, admin actions).

Step 5. Regardless of outcome, review the customer's CA policy structure. If foreign sign-ins on unmanaged devices are slipping through, either the policy is scoped too narrowly or the policy itself is misconfigured.
foreign_login_non_compliant_device.example_scenario:
Your customer's CFO appears to sign in successfully from Bangkok at 11:47 PM local time, from a Windows device that isn't enrolled in the customer's Intune. The CFO's normal pattern is sign-ins from Calgary on a managed laptop and an iPhone.

You contact the CFO's executive assistant directly — the CFO is in Bangkok, but should only be using their managed iPhone, not a Windows laptop. The Bangkok hotel's business-center PC was used because the iPhone battery died.

This is a low-severity legitimate exception, but it's a weak point. The CFO's password and an MFA push were just used on a public hotel computer with no device hygiene controls. You'd: revoke that specific session in Entra immediately, reset the CFO's password as a precaution, ask the CFO to re-register MFA from their own iPhone once charged, and follow up with the customer to formalize a travel-laptop policy.

suspicious_risk_detection.what_is_this: Microsoft Entra ID Protection has flagged a sign-in or user as medium or high risk based on machine-learning signals. Risk reasons include unfamiliar sign-in properties, unusual sign-in locations, anonymous IP usage, malicious IP intelligence, leaked credentials, and atypical token patterns. This alert is the surfacing of Microsoft's own risk engine — Panoptica365 is forwarding what Entra already concluded.
suspicious_risk_detection.why_it_matters: Entra's risk engine is one of the better signals in the Microsoft security stack because it's trained on telemetry from billions of sign-ins. When it raises a medium or high risk on one of your customer's users, it's worth taking seriously — the engine doesn't fire lightly. Common high-risk events include leaked credential matches (Microsoft has correlated the password against a known breach dump), token-replay (someone used a stolen session token), and AiTM phishing (adversary-in-the-middle attacks like EvilGinx).
suspicious_risk_detection.attack_vectors:
The risk reason in the alert tells you which attack pattern Entra detected:

- Leaked credentials. The user's password matches a known breach dump. Either the user reused a password across services, or an attacker has the password and is using it.

- Anonymous IP. Sign-in came from Tor, a known VPN exit, or a privacy proxy. Legitimate uses exist but are rare in business contexts.

- Atypical travel / unfamiliar location. Similar to Impossible Travel but covers a wider range of "this doesn't look like the user."

- Token replay. A session token was used from a different location or device than it was originally issued to. Indicates token theft from session-hijacking malware or AiTM phishing.

- Malware-linked IP. Sign-in came from an IP known to be associated with malware or a botnet.
suspicious_risk_detection.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the alert and note the specific risk reason. Different reasons call for different responses.

Step 2. For leaked-credential risk: reset the user's password immediately and force MFA re-registration. Educate the user about password reuse.

Step 3. For token-replay or AiTM risk: revoke all active sessions for the user, reset password, force MFA re-registration with phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2 or Authenticator number-matching), and review whether mail rules, OneDrive activity, or admin actions occurred during the suspect session.

Step 4. For anonymous IP or atypical location: contact the user out-of-band to confirm. If denied, treat as account takeover.

Step 5. After remediation, set the user's risk state back to "confirmed safe" or "confirmed compromised" in Entra so the engine learns from the disposition.
suspicious_risk_detection.example_scenario:
Entra raises a high-risk detection on a finance manager: reason is "token issuer anomaly," indicating that a token was used in a way inconsistent with how it was originally issued. The user reports nothing unusual.

This is a classic AiTM phishing outcome. The user almost certainly entered their credentials and MFA into a fake Microsoft login page (often delivered by a phishing email pretending to be a Teams message or document share), the attacker captured both the password and the session token, and is now using that token from their own infrastructure.

You'd: revoke all active sessions for the user (kills the attacker's stolen token), reset the password, force re-registration of MFA with FIDO2 or Authenticator number-matching, search the user's mailbox for any newly-created inbox rules (forwarding, deletion, hiding from "Microsoft Security"), search OneDrive/SharePoint for any downloads in the past 24 hours, and confirm whether the user clicked anything suspicious in their mail in the past day. Report the phishing email, if found, to Microsoft.

malware_after_delivery.what_is_this: Microsoft Defender for Office 365 has detected malware in an email after that email was already delivered to a user's mailbox. "After delivery" means the email passed initial scanning at the gateway and reached the inbox, but a later scan (or a Zero-Hour Auto Purge action triggered by new threat intel) flagged it as malicious. The email is typically removed automatically by ZAP, but the user may have already opened it or clicked a link.
malware_after_delivery.why_it_matters: An email that landed in an inbox before being flagged is more dangerous than one blocked at the gateway. The user might have already interacted with it — clicked a link, opened an attachment, replied to it. ZAP removes the message, but it can't undo any action the user took before removal. Treat this alert as "the user might already be compromised" and verify rather than assume the cleanup was complete.
malware_after_delivery.attack_vectors:
After-delivery malware detections typically come from one of these patterns:

- A new-strain campaign that wasn't in Microsoft's signature database when the email arrived. Microsoft updated signatures and retroactively flagged the message via ZAP.

- Targeted spear-phishing using a custom payload that gateway sandboxes didn't initially detect. These are often opened by users because the social engineering is good.

- A delayed payload — the email itself is clean, but it links to an external site that was harmless when scanned and turned malicious afterward.
malware_after_delivery.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the recipient(s) and the timestamps. Did the email get delivered to one user or many? When was it delivered, and when was it removed?

Step 2. Confirm whether ZAP successfully removed the message from the mailbox. Check the Defender portal's threat explorer for the cleanup status.

Step 3. Contact the recipient(s) and ask whether they opened the email, clicked any links, downloaded any attachments, or replied. The window between delivery and ZAP removal is your exposure window.

Step 4. If a user clicked a link or opened an attachment: check their device for indicators of compromise (Defender for Endpoint alerts, unusual processes, network connections to suspicious IPs), and consider isolating the device while you investigate.

Step 5. If the email reached many users, treat as a campaign: search the customer's mailboxes for the same sender or subject in the past 7 days, block the sender domain, and consider notifying users to be alert for similar follow-ups.
malware_after_delivery.example_scenario:
An invoice-themed email is delivered to four users in the customer's accounting team at 09:41. At 10:18, Microsoft's threat intel updates and Defender for O365 retroactively flags the email as malware. ZAP removes the message from three of the four mailboxes. The fourth user had already opened the attachment — a Word document that asked them to enable macros.

The user enabled macros. You contact them, isolate their laptop in Defender for Endpoint, run a full Defender scan, and check Defender's process tree for the past hour. The malware tried to download a second-stage payload but was blocked by the customer's network filtering. You'd reset the user's Microsoft 365 password, revoke their sessions, and audit any mail/file activity from that device in the exposure window. Block the sender domain and search the broader user base for any other deliveries from the same sender.

malware_in_sharepoint_onedrive.what_is_this: Microsoft has detected malware inside a file stored in the customer's SharePoint or OneDrive. The file is automatically blocked from being downloaded or shared while the malware flag is in place. Most often the detection comes from Microsoft's behind-the-scenes scanning of files at rest in tenant storage.
malware_in_sharepoint_onedrive.why_it_matters: A malware-infected file in cloud storage is a different problem than malware in email. In cloud storage, the file may have been uploaded directly by a user, synced from an infected device, or shared by an external collaborator. It can spread when other users open or sync it. Knowing where the file came from tells you whether you have a compromised device, a poorly-managed external sharing relationship, or a one-off bad attachment that someone uploaded by mistake.
malware_in_sharepoint_onedrive.attack_vectors:
Three common origins:

- A user's device is infected and the malware was created in a folder that syncs to OneDrive. The OneDrive sync client uploaded it.

- An external collaborator shared a malicious file through a SharePoint site. The customer's users may have downloaded it before detection.

- A user uploaded a file they thought was safe — for example, a PDF or installer downloaded from a third-party site.
malware_in_sharepoint_onedrive.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the file path, the file owner, and the upload timestamp. The owner field tells you where to start the investigation.

Step 2. Check whether the file is in OneDrive or SharePoint, and whether it was uploaded directly by a user or synced from a device.

Step 3. If synced from a device: that device is the source of infection. Run a full Defender scan, isolate the device if Defender for Endpoint is licensed, and review what other files the device has synced recently.

Step 4. Check the file's sharing history. Has it been shared with anyone, internally or externally? If yes, those recipients may have downloaded it before the malware flag.

Step 5. Delete the file (or have the customer delete it). Confirm Microsoft's malware quarantine moved it out of the user-accessible path. Notify any users who interacted with it.
malware_in_sharepoint_onedrive.example_scenario:
A SharePoint document library used by the customer's project team flags a file named "Q2_review_final.zip" as malware. The file was uploaded an hour ago by an external partner who was given Edit access to the library.

You identify the partner organization, contact the customer's project lead to confirm whether the upload was expected, and ask the lead to communicate with the partner about how the file got infected (likely the partner's own machine is compromised). You delete the file, check whether anyone in the customer's org downloaded it before flagging (three people did), and check those three users' devices for any signs of execution. You'd also review the external sharing arrangement — if the partner's environment is compromised, future shared files could carry the same risk.

inbound_malware_blocked.what_is_this: Exchange Online Protection blocked an inbound email containing malware before it reached the recipient's mailbox. The email never landed; the user never saw it. Panoptica365 is reporting that the block happened so you have visibility into what's being thrown at the customer's tenant.
inbound_malware_blocked.why_it_matters: Individually, a blocked malware email is a non-event — EOP did its job. The signal is in the volume and pattern. A small steady stream of blocked malware is normal background noise on the internet; the entire email-borne malware industry depends on hitting many tenants in hopes of finding one without protection. What's worth attention is targeted activity (the same sender hitting many users in the customer's tenant) or campaigns where the malware family or sender domain ends up being something the customer's industry is being specifically targeted with.
inbound_malware_blocked.attack_vectors:
Patterns worth investigating:

- A sustained campaign from a single sender or domain hitting many users at the customer. Suggests the customer is on a target list — possibly because their domain was in a leaked address book.

- A spike that coincides with public news about the customer's industry, a customer's named partner, or a major business event. Often the precursor to follow-up phishing using a different vector.

- The same malware family showing up repeatedly. Microsoft's signature was strong this time but variants slip through; worth knowing the family name in case Defender for Endpoint flags the same family on a device later.
inbound_malware_blocked.what_to_do:
Step 1. Look at the alert's volume context. A single block needs no action. A burst of blocks pointing to many users from one sender deserves a closer look.

Step 2. Check the sender domain. If it's a domain the customer has business with (a vendor, partner, supplier), the legitimate domain may be compromised — the customer's contact at that vendor should be alerted.

Step 3. Check whether the customer has SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configured properly. Missing email authentication is what lets attackers spoof legitimate-looking domains.

Step 4. If the customer is being repeatedly targeted, consider tightening EOP policies (Strict preset) and educating users on the specific lure being used in the campaign.
inbound_malware_blocked.example_scenario:
Over a 24-hour period, EOP blocks 47 inbound emails to 12 different users at the customer, all carrying the same malware family in attachments named like "PO_##.zip" or "Invoice_##.zip". The senders are different across the 47 emails but all use lookalike domains imitating one of the customer's actual suppliers.

This is a targeted campaign exploiting the customer's business relationship with that supplier. The attacker has the customer's address book — likely from a phishing compromise of someone the customer corresponds with. You'd alert the supplier (they may be the source of the leak), confirm DMARC is set to reject for the customer's own domain, brief the customer's accounting team about the campaign so they don't fall for follow-up phishing using a different attachment, and consider applying a tenant transport rule that quarantines inbound mail from lookalike domains.

inbound_phishing_blocked.what_is_this: Exchange Online Protection blocked or junked an inbound email classified as phishing before it reached the recipient. The user never saw it in their inbox (or saw it only in the Junk folder, which most users ignore). Panoptica365 is reporting that the block happened.
inbound_phishing_blocked.why_it_matters: Phishing is the dominant attack vector against small and mid-size Microsoft 365 tenants. EOP's filters catch the bulk of it, and a single blocked phish is rarely interesting. What you're watching for is volume, targeting, and the kind of phish — a credential-harvesting page is more dangerous than a generic spam-style scam. Repeated phishing attempts from the same campaign or against the same users tell you the customer is being actively targeted.
inbound_phishing_blocked.attack_vectors:
Phishing categories that deserve different responses:

- Credential harvesting. Lures the user to a fake Microsoft 365 login page. AiTM kits like EvilGinx capture both password and MFA token. Single-most-common compromise vector.

- Business email compromise (BEC) / invoice fraud. Pretends to be a known vendor or executive, asks for a wire transfer or payment redirect. No links, no attachments — purely social engineering.

- Targeted spear-phish. Personalized to the recipient using public info or stolen address-book data. Higher click rates than mass phishing.

- Generic mass campaign. Untargeted, low-effort. Most blocked phish falls in this category and is noise.
inbound_phishing_blocked.what_to_do:
Step 1. Review the alert volume and target pattern. One blocked phish on a generic-looking lure: ignore. Many blocks against specific high-value users (admins, finance, executives): investigate as targeting.

Step 2. If the lure is a credential-harvesting kit, check whether any of the targeted users may have interacted with similar emails that weren't blocked. Search their mailboxes (and sent items) for related correspondence in the past week.

Step 3. For BEC patterns, alert the named senders and the targeted users directly. Make sure finance/accounting know not to act on email-only payment changes.

Step 4. If a single user is being repeatedly targeted, consider whether they need extra training, a phishing-resistant MFA method, or to be moved to a stricter CA policy.

Step 5. Report the phishing patterns to Microsoft via the Submissions portal so future blocks improve.
inbound_phishing_blocked.example_scenario:
EOP blocks five phishing emails to the customer's CFO over three days. All five impersonate the CEO and ask the CFO to "confirm a wire transfer detail urgently." No links, no attachments, just text. The senders are all different display-name spoofs of the CEO using free-mail addresses.

This is a classic CEO-fraud BEC campaign targeting the customer specifically. You'd brief the CFO and the CEO directly (out-of-band), confirm the customer has DMARC reject configured to prevent legitimate-domain spoofing, recommend the customer institute a verbal-confirmation policy for wire transfers, and consider an Exchange transport rule that adds a banner to any inbound email impersonating the CEO's display name from outside the org. Report the senders to Microsoft.

inbound_spam_blocked.what_is_this: Exchange Online Protection blocked or junked an inbound email classified as spam. Spam in EOP terminology is unsolicited bulk mail that isn't malicious enough to be classified as phishing or malware — newsletters the user didn't sign up for, low-effort marketing, scams that don't quite cross the phishing line. The user never saw it in their inbox (or only in Junk).
inbound_spam_blocked.why_it_matters: Spam blocks are the lowest-priority alert in this set. They're tracked because volume tells a story: a customer suddenly receiving 20x their normal spam volume often correlates with their email address ending up on a fresh spam list, which is a leak indicator. But individual spam blocks are not actionable.
inbound_spam_blocked.attack_vectors:
Spam doesn't have attack vectors in the security sense — it's pure noise. The exception is when spam volume jumps sharply, which can indicate:

- An email address book leaked from somewhere (a partner who got phished, a public document with internal addresses).

- A user's address ended up on a spammer's list because they entered it on a deceptive site.

- A campaign is being sent to your customer's full domain because one user's address was leaked and the spammers are guessing other addresses on the same domain.
inbound_spam_blocked.what_to_do:
Step 1. Don't act on individual spam blocks. They're not security events.

Step 2. If the customer reports a sudden flood, consider tightening EOP's spam thresholds (Strict preset) and reviewing the spam policy's bulk-email threshold (BCL).

Step 3. If specific users are receiving disproportionate spam, those users may have leaked their addresses through deceptive websites, public forms, or leaked-credential services. Educate them.

Step 4. Make sure the customer's tenant has SPF, DKIM, and DMARC set to enforce. Misconfigured DMARC lets spammers spoof your customer's own domain back at their users.
inbound_spam_blocked.example_scenario:
A customer's spam-block count averages around 200 per day. Over a week it climbs to 3,500 per day. You drill into the EOP reports and find that 80% of the new spam is going to one specific user — the customer's HR director, who recently posted on LinkedIn announcing she joined the company.

The spam is the result of address-harvesting from public LinkedIn data, combined with the customer's straightforward email format (firstname.lastname@). You'd let the spam blocker continue doing its job, brief the HR director that the volume will likely subside in a few weeks, confirm her account is on phishing-resistant MFA in case any spam crosses into phishing territory, and review the customer's general spam policy is on Microsoft's recommended Strict preset.

email_threat_volume_spike.what_is_this: Panoptica365 detected an unusually high volume of blocked email threats (malware, phishing, or both combined) in the customer's tenant compared to the customer's own historical baseline. This is a meta-alert: it doesn't fire on individual blocks (those are separate alerts), but on a statistical jump in the daily count.
email_threat_volume_spike.why_it_matters: A volume spike is a campaign-level signal. The individual blocks were all defensive successes, but the spike tells you the customer was specifically targeted by something larger than usual. Often the spike is the visible part of a multi-pronged attack — phish-mail volume goes up while the attacker simultaneously tries credential-stuffing the same users from a different vector. Knowing about the spike lets you batten down the hatches across all vectors at once.
email_threat_volume_spike.attack_vectors:
What a spike usually means:

- A targeted campaign hitting many users in the customer's domain. Often coincides with a public event (the customer was named in news, hit by a leaked partner, posted publicly about an upcoming deal).

- The customer's domain is now on a campaign target list because their address book leaked from somewhere.

- Background internet noise that happens to align — many simultaneous unrelated campaigns. Less interesting but worth distinguishing.
email_threat_volume_spike.what_to_do:
Step 1. Confirm the spike is real (not a baseline glitch). Look at the per-day counts over the past 30 days.

Step 2. Identify which threat category drove the spike: malware, phishing, or spam. The response differs.

Step 3. Look at the recipients. Is the spike concentrated on specific users (targeted) or spread across the tenant (mass campaign)?

Step 4. Check whether the spike correlates with any known event involving the customer — public announcement, partner breach, leaked document.

Step 5. While the spike is active, enable the customer's strictest available email security posture (Defender for O365 Strict preset, Safe Links, Safe Attachments), brief users about the campaign theme, and watch sign-in logs for any successful credential attacks from the same window.
email_threat_volume_spike.example_scenario:
A customer's normal blocked-threat count is around 30 per day combined. Over a Tuesday, the count is 410. Drilling in, 380 of those are phishing emails, all impersonating Microsoft and asking users to verify their credentials, all sent to ~85% of the customer's user base.

This is a credential-harvesting campaign aimed at the customer's full domain. You'd: tighten Safe Links to Strict and ensure click-time verification is on, brief all users about the specific phishing theme, watch Entra sign-in logs over the next 24-48 hours for any unusual sign-in patterns (especially from users who might have clicked despite training), confirm DMARC is set to reject for the customer's domain so the attacker can't spoof legitimate-looking internal mail, and report the campaign indicators to Microsoft.

ual_mass_file_deletion_burst.what_is_this: A user deleted more than the configured threshold (50 by default) of SharePoint or OneDrive files within a 15-minute window. Detected by replaying Unified Audit Log file-deletion events and aggregating per user. Once an alert fires for a user, a 60-minute cooldown suppresses follow-up alerts for the same user to avoid flooding the dashboard during a single incident.
ual_mass_file_deletion_burst.why_it_matters: Mass file deletion in a short window is one of the cleanest indicators of either ransomware (the attacker is deleting originals after encrypting) or insider sabotage (an angry user or recently-fired employee destroying work). It's also occasionally a legitimate cleanup operation — a user clearing out a project folder — but legitimate cleanups rarely come in 50+ files in 15 minutes without warning. Treat as urgent until proven benign.
ual_mass_file_deletion_burst.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- Ransomware. The attacker has compromised the user's account or device, encrypted files locally and synced the encrypted versions, then deleted the originals. The deletion event is the visible tail of the attack.

- Insider sabotage. A user who knows they're being terminated, or has a grievance, is destroying work. Often selective — specific folders or file types — rather than a blanket sweep.

- Legitimate cleanup. A user methodically deleting old files at end-of-project. Usually happens during business hours, with files concentrated in one easily-explained folder.

- Sync-client misconfiguration. A user accidentally moved or deleted a synced folder on their local machine, and OneDrive faithfully replicated the deletion to the cloud.
ual_mass_file_deletion_burst.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the user, the time window, the affected sites/libraries, and the file count. Look at whether deletions are concentrated in one folder or scattered.

Step 2. Contact the user out-of-band immediately. Are they intentionally deleting? Did their device do something they didn't expect?

Step 3. If the user denies the action: treat as account compromise. Reset password, revoke all sessions, force MFA re-registration, isolate the user's device if Defender for Endpoint is licensed.

Step 4. Restore the files from the SharePoint/OneDrive recycle bin — files are recoverable for 93 days by default. Coordinate with the customer to recover what's needed.

Step 5. Check whether other accounts in the same tenant show similar activity in the past 24 hours. Coordinated mass deletions across users is a stronger ransomware indicator.
ual_mass_file_deletion_burst.example_scenario:
On a Saturday at 02:18, a user with no history of weekend activity deletes 340 files across three SharePoint document libraries in 11 minutes. The user's sign-in immediately before the deletion was from an IP in a foreign country.

This is account compromise followed by attempted ransomware or sabotage. You'd: revoke all the user's sessions immediately, reset their password, isolate any of their devices in Defender for Endpoint, restore the deleted files from the SharePoint recycle bin, confirm none of the customer's other accounts show similar activity in the same window, and check whether any new external sharing links or download bursts were created on those files immediately before the deletion (sometimes the attacker exfiltrates first, then deletes).

ual_microsoft_defender_alert.what_is_this: Microsoft Defender XDR (Defender for Endpoint, Defender for Office 365, Defender for Identity, or Defender for Cloud Apps) raised an alert in the customer's tenant, and Panoptica365 is forwarding it. The severity in Panoptica365 matches what Microsoft assigned. Defender alerts are individual events; the related "Microsoft Defender incident" alert covers Microsoft's correlation of multiple alerts into a single attack story.
ual_microsoft_defender_alert.why_it_matters: Defender's alerts are high-quality signals — Microsoft sees telemetry across billions of endpoints and tunes detections aggressively. When Defender raises an alert on one of the customer's devices or accounts, it's worth treating as real until proven otherwise. The challenge is Defender produces many alerts per day in a healthy tenant, and most are informational or low-severity (e.g., suspicious-looking but legitimate scripts, blocked malware in test scenarios). Watch the Medium and High severities; Informational and Low are usually background.
ual_microsoft_defender_alert.attack_vectors:
What Defender alerts cover, depending on which Defender product fired:

- Defender for Endpoint: malware execution, suspicious processes, privilege escalation, lateral movement, data exfiltration patterns.

- Defender for Office 365: phishing clicks (Safe Links time-of-click), malicious attachments executed, ZAP actions on delivered mail.

- Defender for Identity: anomalous AD activity, ticket-granting service abuse, identity-related credential theft.

- Defender for Cloud Apps: anomalous OAuth grants, mass downloads, unusual file-sharing patterns from cloud apps.
ual_microsoft_defender_alert.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the alert in the Defender portal directly — Panoptica365's forwarding is for visibility, but the full investigation lives in Microsoft's tooling.

Step 2. Note the severity. Critical and High need immediate response. Medium needs review during the day. Informational and Low are tracked but often not actioned individually.

Step 3. For endpoint alerts: identify the device, the user, and the suspect process or file. Defender provides a process tree — read it to understand what happened. Consider isolating the device while you investigate.

Step 4. For identity alerts: identify the user, reset password, revoke sessions, review what the user accessed in the suspect window.

Step 5. Once resolved, mark the alert in Defender with a disposition (true positive, false positive, etc.) so the engine learns. Don't leave alerts indefinitely open.
ual_microsoft_defender_alert.example_scenario:
Defender for Endpoint raises a High-severity alert on the customer's marketing director's laptop: "Suspicious credential access activity" — a process tried to read the LSASS process memory, which is the classic Mimikatz pattern for stealing logged-in credentials.

You pull the process tree in Defender. The triggering process is a command-line tool that ran from a Downloads folder, launched by Microsoft Word. Word was opened with a document the user received in email an hour ago. This is a successful spear-phish: macro-enabled document → credential-theft tool.

You'd: isolate the device immediately in Defender for Endpoint, reset the user's Microsoft 365 password and any other accounts they're signed into on that device, revoke their Entra sessions, confirm whether the credential theft succeeded by checking which accounts were active on the device at the moment, run a full anti-malware scan, and search the rest of the customer's tenant for any other deliveries of the original phishing email.

ual_microsoft_defender_incident.what_is_this: Microsoft Defender XDR has correlated multiple individual alerts into a single "incident" representing a single multi-stage attack. An incident links related alerts that share users, devices, IPs, files, or processes — instead of you triaging 8 separate alerts, Microsoft has decided they're one attack and surfaced them together. Panoptica365 forwards the incident notification when it's first created, when its severity escalates, or when new alerts are added to it.
ual_microsoft_defender_incident.why_it_matters: Incidents are the highest-value signal Defender produces. A standalone alert might be background noise; an incident is Microsoft saying "these eight things are connected and form an attack story." Most real attacks are visible as incidents (with a "how the attack progressed" timeline) rather than as scattered individual alerts. Treat incidents as the primary way to triage Defender — a tenant with many alerts but no high-severity incidents may be safe; a tenant with one Critical incident has an active problem.
ual_microsoft_defender_incident.attack_vectors:
Common incident archetypes:

- Credential theft → lateral movement. User clicks a phishing link, credentials stolen, attacker signs in from a new location, accesses files, creates inbox rules. Defender correlates the click event, the new sign-in, and the post-compromise activity.

- Malware execution → command-and-control → exfiltration. Malware lands on a device, contacts a C2 server, starts uploading data. Each step generates an alert and Defender stitches them.

- BEC progression. Account compromise → mailbox rule creation → impersonation email sent → financial fraud attempt.

- Insider data theft. User downloads many files in a short time, sends to external destination, accompanied by suspicious sign-in or device behavior.
ual_microsoft_defender_incident.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the incident in the Defender portal. Read the attack story Microsoft has constructed — this is your starting point.

Step 2. Identify the affected users, devices, and assets. Severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low) tells you how fast.

Step 3. For Critical and High incidents, treat as active intrusion: isolate affected devices, reset affected user passwords, revoke sessions, contain spread.

Step 4. Review what's in scope. The incident's evidence list shows the files, processes, IPs, and users involved. Each one needs disposition.

Step 5. Coordinate with the customer's stakeholders if the customer has a security contact. Document the response actions in Defender's incident notes; close the incident only when all alerts within it are dispositioned.
ual_microsoft_defender_incident.example_scenario:
Defender raises a High-severity incident titled "Possible AiTM phishing attack and inbox manipulation." It bundles four alerts: (1) suspicious URL click in email at 14:20, (2) successful Entra sign-in from new IP at 14:23, (3) inbox rule created auto-deleting all mail containing "Microsoft" or "login" at 14:25, (4) suspicious mass mail-send to 80 external addresses at 14:31.

This is a textbook AiTM compromise: user fell for a phishing kit that captured both password and MFA token, attacker signed in from their own infrastructure, hid security warnings from the user via inbox rule, and used the account to phish the user's contacts. Defender correlated the four events into one incident.

You'd: isolate the user's device, revoke all sessions, reset password, force re-registration of MFA on a phishing-resistant method, delete the malicious inbox rule, audit the sent items for any phish that landed, alert the recipients of the outbound phish, and search the customer's whole tenant for any other users who received the original phishing email.

ual_anomalous_geo_file_access.what_is_this: A user accessed or downloaded a SharePoint or OneDrive file from a country outside the customer's CA-derived allowed list. Panoptica365 suppresses this alert if the underlying sign-in went through a compliant-device CA gate — meaning the user got in via a managed device under a CA policy that explicitly allowed compliant-device override. If the alert fires, the access happened outside both the country allow list AND the compliant-device path.
ual_anomalous_geo_file_access.why_it_matters: File access from an unexpected country is a strong indicator that an account has been compromised. Unlike Impossible Travel (which requires two sign-ins close in time), this fires on any cloud file activity from an unfamiliar geo. Attackers who have stolen credentials often access files from their own country first to assess what's worth stealing — this alert catches that reconnaissance phase.
ual_anomalous_geo_file_access.attack_vectors:
Likely scenarios when this fires:

- Account takeover. The attacker has the user's credentials and is browsing the user's OneDrive or shared SharePoint sites looking for valuable data.

- Stolen session token. The attacker isn't typing the user's password — they captured a session token via AiTM phishing or session-hijacking malware and are reusing it.

- VPN or anonymizer use by the legitimate user. Less common in business contexts but possible for users behind a personal VPN.

- A traveling user accessing files from their personal device while abroad. The CA exemption for compliant device didn't apply because their personal device isn't enrolled.
ual_anomalous_geo_file_access.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the user, the source country, the IP, the file(s) accessed, and the time. Note whether it was a download or just a view.

Step 2. Confirm with the user out-of-band whether they're traveling or using a VPN.

Step 3. If the user denies the activity: treat as account takeover. Reset password, revoke all sessions, force MFA re-registration with phishing-resistant method, and audit what the impostor session accessed (mail, files, admin actions).

Step 4. Check the file sensitivity. If the accessed files contain financial data, customer PII, intellectual property, or contracts, escalate to the customer's security/leadership contact and consider regulatory notification obligations.

Step 5. Review the customer's CA policies. If a compromised account is accessing files from outside allowed countries, the policies need either tighter geo-restriction or stronger device-compliance enforcement.
ual_anomalous_geo_file_access.example_scenario:
On a Thursday afternoon, a sales engineer's account accesses 47 SharePoint files in 22 minutes from an IP in Romania. The engineer is at their desk in Calgary at the same time according to their other Microsoft 365 activity (Teams calls, email replies).

This is an active session-token theft — the attacker has the user's session cookie (likely from AiTM phishing), and is browsing their files in parallel with the legitimate user being unaware. The user's normal Calgary activity is the legitimate user; the Romania access is the attacker.
