You'd: revoke all the user's sessions immediately (kills the attacker's stolen token), reset the user's password, force re-registration on FIDO2 or Authenticator number-matching, search the customer's audit log for what the attacker accessed and downloaded, identify any sensitive files in that list and notify the customer's leadership, and search the user's mailbox for any newly-created inbox rules the attacker may have planted to hide their activity.

enterprise_application_creation.what_is_this: A new enterprise application has been registered in the customer's Entra tenant. Enterprise applications represent third-party services that have been granted permissions to interact with the tenant — examples are SaaS products, custom-built apps, integration platforms, and service principals used by automation. Each new app is a new identity that can act inside the tenant.
enterprise_application_creation.why_it_matters: Enterprise apps are one of the most-abused persistence mechanisms in Microsoft 365. An attacker who compromises any user with permission to create apps (which is most users by default unless restricted) can register a malicious app, grant it Mail or Files permissions, and use it as a backdoor that survives password resets and even MFA re-registrations. App-based persistence is the kind of thing that gets missed in standard incident response because operators look at user accounts, not app principals.
enterprise_application_creation.attack_vectors:
Watch for these patterns:

- Consent phishing setup. The attacker registers an app and prepares to phish users into consenting to its permissions. The app's name will often impersonate a familiar service ("Microsoft 365 Backup," "Office Sync," etc.).

- Persistence after credential theft. The attacker has compromised a user, and their first move is to register an app with broad delegated permissions so they can access mail/files even after the user changes their password.

- Legitimate SaaS onboarding. The customer is trying out a new tool and the user clicked through a sign-in-with-Microsoft flow.

- Internal automation. A developer at the customer registered the app for a script or integration.
enterprise_application_creation.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify who created the app and when. The audit log carries the operator UPN.

Step 2. Look at the app's display name, publisher, and reply URLs. A fishy publisher (a personal email domain, a recently-registered domain) or a suspicious display name (impersonating Microsoft) is a strong tell.

Step 3. Check the permissions the app has been granted. Mail.ReadWrite, Files.ReadWrite.All, full_access_as_app, Directory.ReadWrite.All — these are high-impact and should never be granted to an app unless the customer's security contact knows about it.

Step 4. Confirm with the operator out-of-band whether they intentionally registered the app.

Step 5. If the app is unauthorized: disable it (set enabled=false), revoke any consent grants, and review what the app accessed since creation via the audit log. If it accessed sensitive data, follow incident-response procedures.
enterprise_application_creation.example_scenario:
At 22:14 on a Wednesday, a user who's a senior accountant at the customer registers an enterprise app named "Microsoft Backup Service" with Mail.ReadWrite.All and Files.ReadWrite.All delegated permissions. The user has no developer background and shouldn't be registering apps after hours.

This is consent-phishing setup post-compromise. The attacker has the user's credentials (likely from AiTM phishing earlier in the day) and is establishing app-based persistence. The app name impersonates Microsoft to make the consent request look legitimate when the attacker tries to phish other users into granting it.

You'd: disable the app immediately, revoke any consent grants made to it, reset the user's password and revoke their sessions, audit what the app accessed since creation (probably nothing yet — it was just registered), search the audit log for any other apps registered by the customer's users in the past 7 days that could be related, and tighten the customer's app-registration policy to require admin approval for new app registrations going forward.

mfa_disabled_users.what_is_this: Panoptica365 detected one or more users in the customer's tenant who do not have Multi-Factor Authentication enforced. "Disabled" here is broad — it covers users with no MFA registration, users excluded from MFA-requiring CA policies, and users on legacy per-user MFA who are toggled off. Because MFA is the single most-effective protection against credential attacks, any user without it is a soft target.
mfa_disabled_users.why_it_matters: Microsoft has stated that MFA blocks over 99% of credential attacks. A user without MFA is the unlocked door an attacker walks through after a password leak or successful phish. In a tenant with universal MFA, an attacker has to get past both the password and the MFA prompt; in a tenant with even one MFA-disabled user, the attacker has a reliable single-factor entry point that they'll find quickly via spray attacks. This alert exists because MFA gaps are still common in MSP-managed tenants, often as legacy exclusions that were never cleaned up.
mfa_disabled_users.attack_vectors:
Why a user might be without MFA:

- Legacy exclusion. The user was excluded years ago for a specific app or device that didn't support MFA, and the exclusion was never reviewed.

- A new user wasn't included in the CA policies that enforce MFA. Often happens with new hires, contractors, or service accounts.

- A break-glass admin account that's intentionally exempt. These are valid but should be documented, separately monitored, and protected by phishing-resistant credentials and a stricter CA policy.

- A deliberately disabled MFA on a recently-compromised account. Attackers who gain Global Admin sometimes disable MFA on accounts they want easier access to.
mfa_disabled_users.what_to_do:
Step 1. List the affected users. Note their roles, last sign-in date, and whether they're active or stale accounts.

Step 2. Stale accounts (no sign-in in 90+ days): consider disabling rather than enrolling. Reduces attack surface.

Step 3. Active accounts: enroll them. Use security defaults or CA policy depending on the tenant. Communicate with the user before forcing enrollment so they don't get blocked at next sign-in.

Step 4. Break-glass accounts: confirm they're documented, on FIDO2 or strong long-passphrase, monitored separately, and excluded only from "normal" MFA policies — not from sign-in entirely.

Step 5. If the lack of MFA appears to be the result of a recent CA policy change, review the change history and revert if unauthorized. An attacker disabling MFA via admin account is a much higher-priority incident than a legacy gap.
mfa_disabled_users.example_scenario:
Panoptica365 reports five users without MFA in a customer's tenant. You review: three are active service-style accounts used by automation (printer service, scanner service, an old API integration), one is an executive who excluded themselves from MFA two years ago because they "hate the prompt," and one is a recent contractor who was given an account but never enrolled.

For the service accounts: replace with proper service principals if possible, or rotate passwords to long random strings, store them in the customer's secret vault, and confirm the accounts have no licensing they don't need. For the executive: enroll on Authenticator with number-matching (the modern UX is much better than legacy MFA prompts) and have a frank conversation about the risk. For the contractor: enroll, and update onboarding to include MFA enrollment in the day-one checklist.

user_password_changes.what_is_this: A user changed their own password (self-service password change) in the customer's tenant. This is normal user behavior — Panoptica365 surfaces it because the *pattern* of password changes can reveal compromise activity, even though individual changes are benign.
user_password_changes.why_it_matters: An attacker who has compromised an account often changes the password as one of their first moves to lock the legitimate user out. The legitimate user, finding their password no longer works, contacts IT to reset — by which time the attacker has already done their damage. This alert lets you catch the password change at the moment it happens, before the legitimate user even realizes they're locked out.
user_password_changes.attack_vectors:
Patterns that distinguish suspicious from routine:

- A password change immediately after a sign-in from an unusual location. The attacker just got in and is establishing control.

- Multiple password changes in quick succession across different users in the same tenant. Unusual unless the customer's IT just rotated all credentials.

- A password change followed by inbox rule creation, mass file access, or external mail forwarding. Standard post-compromise checklist.

- A user's password change on a device or browser they don't normally use. Most users change passwords on the same device they use daily.
user_password_changes.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the user, the source IP/device of the change, and any nearby activity in the audit log.

Step 2. If the source matches the user's normal pattern (their usual office IP or home IP, their managed device), the change is likely legitimate.

Step 3. If the source is unfamiliar, contact the user out-of-band (call or text — don't email, the attacker may control the inbox). Did they just change their password?

Step 4. If the user denies: treat as account takeover. Have an admin reset the password, revoke all sessions, force MFA re-registration with phishing-resistant method.

Step 5. Review the user's activity in the past 60 minutes — inbox rules, file access, admin actions. The attacker has likely already done what they came to do; identify and undo it.
user_password_changes.example_scenario:
At 03:42 on a Sunday, a customer's accounts-payable clerk's password is changed. The change is initiated from an IP address in a country where the customer has no operations and no traveling employees. The clerk has been sleeping for hours.

This is account takeover. The attacker has the password (likely from a leaked credential pair tested via password spray earlier), used it to sign in, and is changing the password to lock out the legitimate user before the user notices.

You'd: have an admin reset the clerk's password immediately (kicks the attacker out), revoke all the clerk's active sessions in Entra, force MFA re-registration on a phishing-resistant method, and audit the past hour of the clerk's activity for inbox rules created (likely yes — to hide "Your password was changed" notifications), file access, and any external mail. Notify the clerk through phone/text channels first thing in the morning.

global_admin_privilege_elevation.what_is_this: A user was elevated to the Global Administrator role in the customer's Entra tenant. Global Admin is the most powerful role in Microsoft 365 — a user with this role can do anything in the tenant: change any other user's password, exempt themselves from any policy, access any data, register apps with any permissions, and disable security tooling. Any new Global Admin assignment is a major event.
global_admin_privilege_elevation.why_it_matters: Global Admin assignment is one of the most consequential changes possible in a tenant. Most tenants should have very few Global Admins (Microsoft recommends 2-4, kept on phishing-resistant MFA, used only for tasks that require GA). An unexpected GA assignment is either a serious misconfiguration (someone elevated a user who shouldn't be) or an active intrusion (attacker has compromised an existing admin and is establishing their own admin presence). Treat as critical until proven legitimate.
global_admin_privilege_elevation.attack_vectors:
What an unexpected GA elevation usually means:

- Active intrusion. An attacker has compromised an existing GA and is creating a backup admin account they control. Common after a successful AiTM phish on an admin.

- Misconfiguration. The customer's IT decided someone needed elevated rights and granted GA instead of a more appropriate scoped role (Exchange Admin, User Admin, etc.).

- Legitimate but undocumented. A new admin was hired and given GA without notice.
global_admin_privilege_elevation.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the new GA, the operator who made the assignment, and the time. The operator UPN tells you who to ask.

Step 2. Verify with the operator out-of-band that they intentionally made the assignment. Use phone, in-person, or a separate channel — not email or Teams chat (which the attacker may control).

Step 3. If the assignment is legitimate but the new GA shouldn't have been GA: replace with the appropriate scoped role. Most admin tasks don't need GA.

Step 4. If the operator denies the assignment: treat as critical incident. Reset the operator's password, revoke their sessions, demote the unauthorized GA, audit the operator's activity in the past 24 hours, and engage the customer's leadership/security contact.

Step 5. Confirm the new GA is on phishing-resistant MFA before the elevation effectively activates them. GAs without FIDO2 or Authenticator number-matching are a significant risk.
global_admin_privilege_elevation.example_scenario:
On a Wednesday at 16:45, the customer's existing Global Admin (an MSP technician's named account) appears to elevate a regular user — a junior accountant — to Global Admin role. The MSP technician is on vacation that week.

The technician's account has been compromised. The attacker (using the technician's stolen credentials) is creating a parallel GA they can use even after the technician returns and changes their password. The junior accountant is unaware their account is being weaponized.

You'd: immediately demote the junior accountant from GA, reset both the technician's and the accountant's passwords, revoke all sessions on both accounts, force phishing-resistant MFA registration on the technician's account, audit the past 24 hours of the technician's actions in the Entra audit log for any other unauthorized changes (inbox rules, app registrations, MFA tampering on other admins, CA policy changes), notify the customer's leadership and any other admins, and consider whether the customer needs broader incident-response engagement.

external_user_addition.what_is_this: A guest user (B2B collaboration user from outside the customer's tenant) was added to the customer's tenant. Guest accounts are normal in tenants that collaborate with partners, contractors, or auditors — but each new guest is an identity with at least some access to the tenant, so additions are tracked.
external_user_addition.why_it_matters: Guest accounts are a common path for attackers to gain a foothold in a tenant. An attacker who compromises a partner organization's user can leverage their B2B guest invitation to access the customer's tenant via the guest pattern, often with surprising amounts of access if the customer's external collaboration permissions are loose. Tracking guest additions lets you confirm each one is legitimate and appropriately scoped.
external_user_addition.attack_vectors:
Patterns to watch for:

- A guest invited from an unfamiliar domain, with no apparent business reason.

- A guest invited and immediately given elevated permissions (admin role, sensitive group membership, file access).

- A burst of guest invitations from a single user's account — unusual unless the user is on a known onboarding role.

- Guests from free-mail domains (gmail, outlook.com, hotmail) when the customer's normal partners use corporate domains.
external_user_addition.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the inviter, the invited email address, and the time. The audit log carries the operator UPN.

Step 2. Check the invited domain. Familiar partner? Contractor's company? Free-mail address? Each tells a different story.

Step 3. Confirm with the inviter out-of-band that the invitation was intentional and appropriate.

Step 4. Check what the guest has been added to — direct group memberships, role assignments, specific SharePoint access. A guest with just "Member" status and no group memberships is low-risk; a guest in a privileged group needs immediate review.

Step 5. If unauthorized: remove the guest, revoke any access they've used, audit what they accessed since invitation, and reset the inviter's password if their account appears compromised.
external_user_addition.example_scenario:
At 23:18 on a Friday, a junior employee's account invites three guest users — all from random gmail addresses — and adds them to a SharePoint group that has access to the customer's payroll documents.

This is post-compromise data exfiltration setup. The attacker (using the junior employee's stolen credentials) is creating accounts they control, granting them access to payroll, and likely about to download from those guest sessions to obscure the trail.

You'd: immediately remove the three guests, audit any file access they've already performed, reset the junior employee's password and revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration, audit the rest of the employee's activity in the past 24 hours for anything else suspicious, notify the customer's HR/leadership about the payroll access (might require regulatory/employee notification), and review the customer's external sharing settings to tighten who can invite guests.

oauth_consent_grant.what_is_this: A user (or admin on behalf of users) granted OAuth permissions to an application. This means the user clicked "Yes, allow this app to access my account" on a Microsoft consent prompt, and the app now has the permissions it requested — most commonly Mail, Files, or Directory access. The most dangerous form of attack against Microsoft 365 over the past few years has been consent phishing: tricking users into granting attacker-controlled apps permission to read their mail and files.
oauth_consent_grant.why_it_matters: Consent phishing bypasses MFA. Once a user grants an app Mail.ReadWrite, the attacker doesn't need the user's password again — they can just use the app's token to read every email forever. The user consented to it, the consent is legitimate from Microsoft's perspective, and the attacker has persistent access that survives password resets, MFA changes, and even some forms of session revocation. The only way to break the access is to revoke the consent, which most users don't know how to do.
oauth_consent_grant.attack_vectors:
Common consent-phishing patterns:

- An app with a name impersonating Microsoft ("Office365 Mail Sync," "Microsoft Backup," "Outlook Plus") asks for Mail.ReadWrite.

- An app from a personal-domain publisher with broad scopes — Mail, Files, Directory.

- A mass-consent campaign where multiple users at the customer get tricked into consenting on the same day.

- High-risk scope combinations: Mail.* + Files.Read.All + offline_access (the offline_access scope makes the access persistent).

- Admin consent granted to an app that should have only had user-scope consent.
oauth_consent_grant.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the user, the app, and the scopes granted. App display name, publisher, reply URLs, and consent scopes are all in the alert.

Step 2. Look up the app in Microsoft's app catalog (or via the app's publisher info). Known good apps from major publishers are usually safe; unknown apps from sketchy publishers are red flags.

Step 3. Check the scopes. Mail.Read or Mail.ReadWrite + offline_access on an app you don't recognize is consent phishing until proven otherwise.

Step 4. If the app is malicious: revoke the consent immediately (Entra → Enterprise Apps → the app → Permissions → Remove), disable the app entirely, audit what the app accessed via the sign-in logs for that app principal.

Step 5. Educate the user about consent phishing. Consider tightening the customer's app-consent policy to require admin approval for any app requesting sensitive scopes.
oauth_consent_grant.example_scenario:
At 11:24 on a Monday, six users at the customer all consent to an app named "Office Mail Backup" within a 14-minute window. The app's publisher is "officemailbackup.example.com," registered three days ago. The app requests Mail.ReadWrite, Files.Read.All, and offline_access.

This is a consent-phishing campaign. The attacker sent a phishing email earlier in the morning that looked like a legitimate Microsoft notification ("Confirm your mailbox migration"), with a link to the consent screen. Users clicked through, granted the permissions, and the attacker now has persistent access to their mail and files.

You'd: revoke consent for all six users on that app immediately, disable and delete the app from the tenant, audit what the app accessed since the first consent (probably mail-reading and possibly bulk-downloading), search all the customer's users for the original phishing email and remove it via ZAP, brief affected users about what happened and what data may have been accessed, and tighten the customer's app-consent policy so future user-consent on sensitive scopes requires admin approval.

admin_role_assignment.what_is_this: A user was assigned to an administrative role in the customer's Entra tenant. This is a broader category than the Global Admin alert — it covers any privileged role assignment including Exchange Admin, SharePoint Admin, Security Admin, User Admin, Helpdesk Admin, and others. The Global Admin alert fires separately because GA is uniquely powerful.
admin_role_assignment.why_it_matters: Privileged roles let users take actions that affect security, data, or other users. An unexpected role assignment is either a misconfiguration or a sign of post-compromise admin lateral movement. Treat all privileged assignments as something to verify, not as routine. Some roles (Privileged Auth Admin, Privileged Role Admin, Application Admin) are particularly dangerous because they can be used to escalate further or grant other roles.
admin_role_assignment.attack_vectors:
Common patterns:

- Active intrusion. An attacker who has compromised an admin is granting privileged roles to other accounts they control, building a backup network of compromised identities.

- Privilege creep. Users get granted admin roles for one-time tasks and the roles are never removed. The customer's tenant accumulates many admins over time, each a separate target.

- New employee onboarding. A new hire was given an admin role as part of their job (legitimate, but worth confirming).

- Misuse of broad roles. A user was given Global Admin or Privileged Role Admin when a narrower role would have done the job.
admin_role_assignment.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the new admin, the role, the operator who assigned it, and the time. Note which role specifically — the response varies.

Step 2. Verify with the operator out-of-band that the assignment is intentional and appropriate.

Step 3. If legitimate but the role is too broad: replace with a narrower role (e.g., User Admin instead of Global Admin for password resets).

Step 4. If the operator denies: treat as compromise. Reset the operator's password, demote the new admin, audit recent changes, escalate.

Step 5. Confirm the new admin is on phishing-resistant MFA before they start using the role. Privileged accounts on SMS-MFA are too soft for admin work.
admin_role_assignment.example_scenario:
On a Sunday morning, a customer's Helpdesk Admin (a junior MSP technician's account) appears to assign Privileged Role Admin to a regular employee's account. The technician is sleeping; the assignment came from an IP in a country the technician has never worked from.

This is post-compromise privilege escalation. The attacker has the junior technician's credentials (likely from earlier credential theft), and is using the technician's existing rights to assign Privileged Role Admin — a role that lets them grant any other role, including Global Admin — to a separate account they control. The chain: junior tech → Helpdesk Admin → grants PRA → uses PRA to grant GA → game over.

You'd: immediately demote the regular employee from PRA, reset the technician's password and revoke their sessions, force MFA re-registration on phishing-resistant method, audit the past 24 hours of the technician's activity for any other privileged changes, notify the customer and any other admins on the tenant about the incident, and consider engaging incident-response support if the attacker had already escalated further.

ual_mailbox_permission_added.what_is_this: A mailbox permission (Full Access, Send-As, or SendOnBehalf) was granted to a user other than the mailbox owner. Detected from the Unified Audit Log, so the alert carries the operator UPN, the grantee, the access rights, and the source IP. Mailbox permissions let one user read or send mail as another user — useful for delegation but heavily abused for impersonation.
ual_mailbox_permission_added.why_it_matters: Mailbox permissions are a stealth path to mail access. Unlike inbox forwarding (which makes copies of mail visible to the receiver), mailbox-level Full Access lets a user open the mailbox like they own it — read everything, search history, send replies. Attackers love mailbox-level access because it persists through password changes (it's a permission, not a credential) and it doesn't trigger sign-in alerts on the target account. If an attacker gets admin rights, granting themselves Full Access to executive mailboxes is a common move.
ual_mailbox_permission_added.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- Post-compromise mail-access setup. An attacker with admin rights grants Full Access on high-value mailboxes (executives, finance) to a user account they control.

- Legitimate delegation. An executive wants their assistant to manage their inbox. Common and benign.

- Send-As setup for impersonation. The attacker grants Send-As to one user so they can send email appearing to come from another (often an executive) for BEC.
ual_mailbox_permission_added.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the operator (who granted), the grantee (who got access), the mailbox (whose mail is now accessible), the right (Full Access, Send-As, SendOnBehalf), and the time.

Step 2. Confirm with the mailbox owner out-of-band that they're aware of and approve the delegation. The mailbox owner is the right person to confirm — not the operator who granted it.

Step 3. If the mailbox owner denies authorization: revoke the permission immediately, treat as account compromise on the operator's account, and audit what the grantee has accessed in the mailbox since.

Step 4. For Send-As specifically: search the mailbox sent-items for any messages sent by the grantee in the past 24 hours. Impersonation attacks move fast.

Step 5. Review the customer's general mailbox-permission posture. A tenant with many mailbox delegations should have an audit of who has access to whose mail; surprises here usually mean unauthorized or forgotten grants.
ual_mailbox_permission_added.example_scenario:
On a Wednesday at 10:42, the customer's IT helper account grants Full Access to the CEO's mailbox to a user who's listed as a marketing intern. The CEO is on a phone call at the time and has no idea.

This is post-compromise mail-access setup. The attacker has the IT helper's credentials and is using them to grant themselves access to the CEO's mailbox via the marketing-intern account they've separately compromised. The grant is legitimate-looking from Microsoft's perspective; it's only suspicious because of the role mismatch (marketing intern shouldn't have CEO mailbox access) and the timing (no business reason for the change).

You'd: revoke the Full Access grant immediately, search the CEO mailbox for any access by the intern's account in the past hour (likely yes — emails opened, possibly drafts created), reset both the IT helper's and the marketing intern's passwords and revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration, audit what other mailbox grants the IT helper made in the past 24 hours, and notify the customer's leadership.

ual_oauth_consent_app_role_grant.what_is_this: A user or admin granted OAuth permissions or assigned an app role to an application. Similar to the polling-based OAuth Consent Grant alert, but this one comes from the Unified Audit Log so it captures the event in near-real-time and carries the operator IP and detailed scope info. Severity escalates when high-risk scopes are involved (Mail.*, Files.Read.All, offline_access, full_access_as_app, Directory.*.All) or when the consent was admin-granted.
ual_oauth_consent_app_role_grant.why_it_matters: Same fundamentals as the polling OAuth alert — consent phishing is one of the most-effective attacks against modern Microsoft 365 tenants because it bypasses MFA. The UAL version of the alert exists because UAL surfaces the event faster than the polling-based detection (often within minutes vs. up to an hour) and carries richer context (operator IP, exact scope strings, whether admin-consented). Treat as the same threat as the polling-based version, just earlier.
ual_oauth_consent_app_role_grant.attack_vectors:
Same patterns as the polling alert: consent phishing, mass-consent campaigns, persistence apps, scope-overprivileged business apps. The UAL-enriched version is more useful for two specific scenarios:

- Admin consent. An admin granted consent on behalf of all users at once. This is high-impact because it bypasses individual user choice. Should be rare and always pre-approved.

- Scope escalation. An app already had permissions and an admin just granted it more. Often happens via app management automation — but also a path for an attacker who has admin to escalate an app they already control.
ual_oauth_consent_app_role_grant.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the app, the operator, the IP, and the exact scopes granted. The audit log's specifics matter here.

Step 2. If admin-consented: critical. Verify with the admin out-of-band. Admin consent should never come as a surprise.

Step 3. Check the app's publisher, reply URLs, and any existing consents — is this a new app or an existing app getting more permissions?

Step 4. If the consent looks malicious (suspicious app, surprising admin, sketchy publisher): revoke immediately, disable the app, audit what the app has accessed since.

Step 5. Tighten the customer's consent policy. For tenants without restrictive policy, even non-admin users can grant Mail/Files scopes — this should be locked down to admin-approval-required.
ual_oauth_consent_app_role_grant.example_scenario:
At 02:18 on a Saturday, the customer's Global Admin account grants admin-consent to an app named "Microsoft Backup Helper" with Mail.ReadWrite.All, Files.ReadWrite.All, and Directory.Read.All scopes. The Global Admin is asleep.

This is post-compromise admin-consent abuse. The attacker has compromised the GA (likely through AiTM phishing earlier), and is using the GA's privilege to grant a malicious app full read/write access to all mail and files in the tenant on behalf of every user. The app now has tenant-wide access that survives the GA's password reset.

You'd: revoke the admin consent immediately, disable and delete the app, reset the GA's password and revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration on phishing-resistant method, audit everything the app has accessed since consent (likely already exfiltrated mail or files), search the audit log for any other apps the GA may have granted in the same window, notify the customer's leadership about the breach scope, and consider professional incident-response engagement.

ual_service_principal_credentials_added.what_is_this: A new client secret or certificate was added to a service principal or application registration. Service principals are the non-human identities that apps use to authenticate to Microsoft 365 — like passwords for apps. Adding a new credential to an existing SP is one of the cleanest persistence techniques because it survives even if the original credentials are rotated.
ual_service_principal_credentials_added.why_it_matters: Once an attacker has any admin rights, adding a credential to an existing service principal gives them long-lived programmatic access that doesn't appear in user audit logs and doesn't require any user to interact. The SP runs as itself — it doesn't trigger sign-in alerts, doesn't get password-reset notifications, and won't be caught by user-focused MFA enforcement. Detecting credential additions is one of the few ways to catch this technique before the attacker uses it.
ual_service_principal_credentials_added.attack_vectors:
Why an attacker would add an SP credential:

- Persistence. The attacker had GA briefly, added a credential to a high-permission SP, and now has indefinite access to whatever the SP can do — often Mail.*, Files.*, or Directory.* across the whole tenant.

- Stealth. SP-based access doesn't tickle user-focused detections. Even if the GA's password is reset and MFA re-enrolled, the SP credential keeps working.

- Legitimate automation. The customer's IT or a developer added a new credential because the old one was about to expire or rotated for security.
ual_service_principal_credentials_added.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the SP, the operator who added the credential, the credential type (secret vs. certificate), and the time.

Step 2. Look at the SP's permissions. An SP with no permissions getting a new credential is low-impact; an SP with Mail or Files or Directory permissions getting a new credential is critical.

Step 3. Confirm with the operator out-of-band that the credential addition was intentional. Real automation rotations are usually documented and predictable.

Step 4. If unauthorized: remove the credential, disable the SP, audit what the SP has accessed since the credential was added.

Step 5. Review the customer's general SP posture. SPs should have minimum necessary permissions, credentials should rotate on a schedule, and unused SPs should be disabled.
ual_service_principal_credentials_added.example_scenario:
At 04:11 on a Saturday, a service principal that was originally created for a Microsoft 365 backup integration gets a new client secret added. The SP has Mail.ReadWrite.All and Files.ReadWrite.All app-only permissions. The operator who added the credential is the customer's GA — at the same time the GA had also just consented to a separate suspicious app (per a coincident OAuth consent alert).

This is multi-pronged persistence setup. The attacker has compromised the GA and is establishing two backup paths: a malicious new app with admin consent (per the other alert) AND a fresh credential on an existing high-permission SP that was already in the tenant for legitimate reasons. The existing SP looks innocuous in any review because it predates the breach.

You'd: remove the new credential immediately, disable the SP entirely while you investigate, treat the GA as fully compromised (reset, revoke, re-enroll MFA), audit every action the GA has taken in the past 48 hours, search for other newly-added SP credentials and newly-consented apps, and engage IR support — multiple persistence mechanisms suggest a sophisticated attacker.

ual_privileged_role_assignment.what_is_this: A user was assigned an Entra privileged role via a UAL-detected event. This is the same security event as the polling-based Admin Role Assignment alert, but surfaced in near-real-time from the Unified Audit Log with operator UPN, IP, and exact role name. Severity is computed per-role: severe for Global Admin, Privileged Role Admin, Privileged Auth Admin, and Security Admin; high for Application Admin, Cloud App Admin, Exchange Admin, SharePoint Admin, and Teams Admin.
ual_privileged_role_assignment.why_it_matters: Same fundamentals as the polling Admin Role Assignment alert. The UAL version exists because the audit-log-based detection fires within minutes (faster than polling) and carries operator IP info that helps you decide whether the assignment looks suspicious. The role-specific severity also matters: Privileged Role Admin and Privileged Auth Admin can be used to escalate further (PRA can grant any role; PAA can change anyone's MFA), so they're treated more critically than role-specific admins.
ual_privileged_role_assignment.attack_vectors:
Same as the polling version: post-compromise lateral movement, misconfigured onboarding, privilege creep, undocumented hires. The role-tier-specific severity points to the most critical patterns:

- Severe-tier roles (GA, PRA, PAA, Security Admin). Any unauthorized assignment is incident-grade. PRA + PAA in particular let the holder escalate further — these are the roles attackers want.

- High-tier roles (App Admin, Exchange Admin, etc.). Service-specific power; an attacker with Exchange Admin can read or modify mail/transport across the tenant without GA.
ual_privileged_role_assignment.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the new admin, the operator, the role, and the source IP. Note specifically which role tier.

Step 2. Verify with the operator out-of-band. Operator IP that doesn't match their normal pattern is a strong tell.

Step 3. For severe-tier roles (GA, PRA, PAA, Security Admin) on unauthorized assignment: treat as critical incident. Demote, reset operator credentials, audit, escalate.

Step 4. For high-tier roles on unauthorized assignment: still significant. Demote, investigate.

Step 5. Confirm new admin is on phishing-resistant MFA. Privileged accounts on SMS or voice MFA are unacceptable.
ual_privileged_role_assignment.example_scenario:
At 13:22 on a Tuesday, a regular user is assigned Privileged Auth Admin role by the customer's Global Admin. The GA is in a video call at the moment. The role assignment came from an IP in a foreign country.

This is GA compromise with deliberate privilege escalation. PAA lets the holder change any user's MFA — including the GA's. The attacker's plan: now that they have PAA, they can disable the real GA's MFA and lock them out, or add their own MFA method to the GA's account so they keep access even after a password reset.

You'd: demote the regular user from PAA immediately, reset the GA's password and revoke all their sessions, audit the GA's MFA methods (the attacker may have already added one), reset MFA enrollment to the GA's known-good methods, audit the past few hours for any other privileged changes the GA has made, force phishing-resistant MFA, and treat this as a major incident requiring leadership notification.

ual_admin_initiated_password_reset.what_is_this:
An admin reset another user's password (operator UPN ≠ target UPN). Self-password-changes don't fire this alert; they fire the User Password Changes alert. This one specifically surfaces helpdesk-style password resets where one user (an admin) changed another user's password.

If the action matches an MSP audit-log entry within ±15 minutes (operator using Panoptica365's helpdesk function), the alert is auto-attributed to that operator and the alert is downgraded to informational. Unattributed admin resets surface as full alerts.
ual_admin_initiated_password_reset.why_it_matters: Admin-initiated password resets are dual-use. They're routine in any business — users forget passwords, admins help. They're also the standard playbook for an attacker who has compromised an admin account and wants to take over more users. Because the same action covers both, the only way to distinguish is to verify the admin was supposed to do it. Auto-attribution to MSP audit log handles the routine cases automatically; what's left in alerts is everything that didn't match expected operator activity.
ual_admin_initiated_password_reset.attack_vectors:
What an unattributed admin password reset usually means:

- A compromised admin is taking over additional users by resetting their passwords. The classic post-compromise lateral-movement move.

- An MSP technician forgot to log the change in their helpdesk system, so attribution didn't match. Annoying but benign.

- A new admin was hired who hasn't been integrated into the MSP audit-log workflow yet.
ual_admin_initiated_password_reset.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the operator (who reset), the target (whose password was reset), and the source IP/time of the operator's session.

Step 2. Verify with the operator out-of-band that they intentionally reset the target's password.

Step 3. If the operator confirms: log the action in the MSP audit system retroactively if appropriate, so future similar actions auto-attribute. Move on.

Step 4. If the operator denies: critical. Reset the operator's password and revoke their sessions, audit recent admin activity from the operator's account, reset the target's password again from a known-clean admin (the attacker may have set a password they know), notify the target user that their password was tampered with.

Step 5. Review whether the target user's account had any post-reset activity in the audit log — that's what the attacker did with the new password.
ual_admin_initiated_password_reset.example_scenario:
At 21:34 on a Sunday, the customer's Helpdesk Admin (an MSP junior tech account) resets the password for a senior accountant. The reset is not in the MSP audit log. The operator's source IP is a foreign country the technician has never worked from.

This is admin compromise being used for lateral takeover. The attacker has the junior tech's credentials, is using their Helpdesk Admin role to reset the senior accountant's password, and will then sign in as the accountant to access financial data.

You'd: reset both the junior tech's password and the senior accountant's password (a clean reset from a verified-clean admin), revoke all sessions on both accounts, force MFA re-registration with phishing-resistant method on both, audit the technician's activity in the past few hours for any other resets they may have done (chain compromise), notify the senior accountant about the incident, and engage IR support if multiple users have been compromised.

ual_mfa_method_tampered_admin_on_behalf_.what_is_this: An admin registered or removed an MFA method (or a security-info item like a phone number or alternate email) on another user's account. Self-changes (where the operator and target are the same user) are suppressed — those are routine. Admin-on-behalf-of changes are tracked because they're the way an attacker with admin rights would establish persistent MFA control over a target. Severity is severe on removal; the alert auto-attributes to MSP audit-log entries within ±15 minutes when matched.
ual_mfa_method_tampered_admin_on_behalf_.why_it_matters: MFA method tampering is the closing move in account-takeover playbooks. After an attacker compromises an admin, they can register their own phone number as an MFA method on a target user's account. Now even if the target's password is reset, the attacker still receives the MFA codes — they have the second factor, they're effectively the user. Removal is even worse: stripping MFA off a user's account leaves it password-only, which is the soft-target setup for the next stage of attack.
ual_mfa_method_tampered_admin_on_behalf_.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- Account takeover persistence. An attacker with admin rights adds their phone or Authenticator to a high-value target's account. Survives password resets.

- Account takeover preparation. An attacker removes the legitimate MFA from a target so they can use leaked or password-sprayed credentials.

- Helpdesk activity. A user lost their phone, an admin removed the old method and registered a new one. Routine. Should be logged in MSP audit and auto-attribute.

- Compromised admin disabling MFA on themselves indirectly via a helper account they've set up.
ual_mfa_method_tampered_admin_on_behalf_.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the operator, the target, the method (registered or removed, what kind), and the time/IP.

Step 2. Verify with the target user out-of-band that the change was authorized. The target is the right person to confirm — they're the one whose access is at stake.

Step 3. If the target denies: treat as critical compromise of the operator. Reset the operator's password and revoke sessions, undo the MFA change on the target, force MFA re-registration on the target with their original methods, and audit what the operator has been doing.

Step 4. For removals specifically: re-enable MFA on the target immediately. Don't leave the account password-only.

Step 5. Consider whether the customer's MFA-methods policy should prevent admin-on-behalf-of registrations — Microsoft has this lockdown available and most customers should use it.
ual_mfa_method_tampered_admin_on_behalf_.example_scenario:
On a Friday at 17:48, the customer's User Admin (an MSP account) registers a new phone number as an Authenticator method on the CFO's account. The phone number doesn't match any number in the customer's records. The User Admin is on PTO — they're at a wedding and not working.

This is User-Admin-account compromise being used to plant persistence on the CFO. The attacker has the User Admin's credentials, registered their own phone number as an MFA method on the CFO's account, and now even if the CFO's password is later reset, the attacker still gets the MFA codes.

You'd: remove the new MFA method from the CFO's account immediately, reset both the User Admin's and the CFO's passwords, revoke all sessions on both, force MFA re-registration with phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2 ideally) on both, audit the User Admin's recent actions for any other MFA tampering on other accounts, notify the customer's leadership, and consider the User Admin role's permissions — User Admin shouldn't be able to manage MFA on privileged users; this should be locked down via Authentication Method policy.

ual_exchange_role_group_membership_chang.what_is_this: A user was added to or removed from an Exchange Online RBAC role group. Exchange role groups are a separate permission system from Entra roles — they govern what users can do inside Exchange (manage transport rules, search mailboxes, perform eDiscovery, etc.). Severe when Organization Management or Discovery Management is touched. These are Exchange's equivalent to Global Admin and Compliance Admin.
ual_exchange_role_group_membership_chang.why_it_matters: Exchange role groups are a known blind spot in security monitoring because they're not Entra roles — many security tools that watch for privileged-role changes miss them. An attacker who can't escalate to Global Admin can sometimes escalate to Organization Management in Exchange, which gives them control over mail flow, transport rules, and mailbox content across the whole tenant. From there they can set up persistent mail forwarding, exempt themselves from audit logging, and read any mailbox.
ual_exchange_role_group_membership_chang.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- Active intrusion. An attacker is escalating into Exchange admin to gain mail-layer control.

- Discovery Management abuse. Someone added themselves or another user to Discovery Management to perform compliance searches that expose mail content tenant-wide.

- Privilege creep. Users get added to role groups for one-off tasks and never removed.

- Legitimate operational change. New admin onboarding, MSP transition.
ual_exchange_role_group_membership_chang.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the role group (especially: Organization Management, Discovery Management, Compliance Management — these are the dangerous ones), the user added/removed, the operator, and the time.

Step 2. Verify with the operator out-of-band that the change is intentional.

Step 3. For sensitive role groups, escalate even if the operator confirms. The role's power means the change deserves a documented business reason.

Step 4. If unauthorized: remove the user from the role group, treat the operator's account as compromised, audit Exchange-side activity (transport rule changes, mailbox searches, mail-flow changes) since the membership change.

Step 5. Make sure the customer's Exchange role group memberships are reviewed periodically. Most tenants accumulate too many members in privileged role groups.
ual_exchange_role_group_membership_chang.example_scenario:
At 12:14 on a Wednesday, a user who is a customer's accounts-receivable clerk is added to the Discovery Management Exchange role group by the Global Admin. Discovery Management lets the holder run mail searches across the entire tenant.

The Global Admin is genuinely the operator who made the change — but they're being prompted by an attacker who has compromised them. The clerk is the attacker's own controlled account; once in Discovery Management, the attacker uses the clerk's identity to run mail searches across the customer's executive mailboxes and exfiltrate the results.

You'd: remove the clerk from Discovery Management immediately, audit the past hour of Discovery searches and exports run by either the GA or the clerk's account, reset both accounts' passwords and revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration with phishing-resistant method, and notify the customer's leadership — sensitive mail content may have been exfiltrated.

ual_site_collection_administrator_added.what_is_this: A user was elevated to Site Collection Administrator on a SharePoint site. SCA is a per-site privilege that lets the holder manage the site's permissions, content, and settings — they can read every file in the site, share anything with anyone, and change who else has access. Site collection admin operates outside the tenant's Entra role hierarchy, which is what makes it interesting for attackers.
ual_site_collection_administrator_added.why_it_matters: SCA bypasses tenant-role detection. An attacker who can't get to Entra Global Admin or SharePoint Admin can sometimes get themselves added as SCA on specific high-value sites — finance team's site, executive document library, HR site — and access everything in scope without ever needing tenant-level admin. Most security tooling watches for role changes at the tenant level; SCA changes happen at the site level and are easy to miss.
ual_site_collection_administrator_added.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- Targeted exfiltration setup. An attacker with some access wants admin-level reach on a specific site (finance, legal, HR) and adds themselves as SCA.

- Legitimate site ownership change. A new project lead is taking over a site; SCA assignment is part of onboarding.

- Misconfiguration. A user was given SCA when they really only needed Edit access on the site.
ual_site_collection_administrator_added.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the new SCA, the site URL, the operator, and the time. Note the site's content category — finance/HR/legal sites are high-value, project sites are usually lower-stakes.

Step 2. Verify with the operator out-of-band that the assignment is intentional. Site owners and tenant SharePoint Admins are the usual operators here.

Step 3. For sensitive sites (finance, legal, HR, executive): escalate even if the operator confirms. Sensitive-site SCAs deserve documented justification.

Step 4. If unauthorized: remove the user from SCA on the site, audit what they accessed since the elevation (downloads, shares created, permissions changes), and treat the operator's account as compromised.

Step 5. Review whether the customer's SharePoint sites have SCA membership reviewed periodically. Many tenants accumulate forgotten SCA assignments.
ual_site_collection_administrator_added.example_scenario:
At 19:51 on a Thursday, a junior IT helper account (the operator) adds a regular employee as Site Collection Administrator on the customer's executive document library — a site that contains M&A documents, board materials, and salary spreadsheets. The IT helper is at home and not working.

This is SCA-based exfiltration setup. The attacker has the IT helper's credentials, used them to grant the regular employee SCA on the executive site, and the regular employee's account is also compromised. From there the attacker logs in as the employee and downloads everything.

You'd: remove the SCA grant immediately, audit the past hour of file access on that site by either account, reset both accounts' passwords and revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration with phishing-resistant method, notify the customer's leadership immediately (sensitive board content may have been accessed), search for other SCA grants the IT helper has made recently, and consider the customer's broader SharePoint admin posture.

ual_send_as_sendonbehalf_permission_gran.what_is_this: A SendAs or SendOnBehalf permission was granted on a mailbox. SendAs lets the grantee send email that appears (to recipients) to come from the mailbox owner, with no "on behalf of" qualifier — pure impersonation. SendOnBehalf is similar but adds an "on behalf of" tag in the From line so recipients can see who actually sent it.
ual_send_as_sendonbehalf_permission_gran.why_it_matters: SendAs is the cleanest impersonation vector inside Microsoft 365. With SendAs on the CEO's mailbox, an attacker can send mail that looks indistinguishable from the CEO's own mail — same display name, same email address, same signature if reused. This is the textbook setup for BEC fraud: send a wire-transfer request "from the CEO" to the CFO, and the CFO has no way to tell from the mail metadata that it isn't real. SendOnBehalf is less abusable because the tag exposes the impersonator, but still risky.
ual_send_as_sendonbehalf_permission_gran.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- BEC setup. Attacker grants themselves SendAs on a high-value mailbox (CEO, CFO) so they can send fraudulent mail that looks legitimate.

- Legitimate delegation. An executive's assistant is being given SendAs to manage correspondence on their behalf.

- Account takeover spreading. An attacker who has admin grants SendAs on multiple mailboxes to maximize their impersonation surface.
ual_send_as_sendonbehalf_permission_gran.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the mailbox owner (whose mail can now be sent as), the grantee (who got the permission), the right (SendAs vs SendOnBehalf), the operator who made the grant, and the time.

Step 2. Confirm with the mailbox owner out-of-band that the delegation is intentional and authorized.

Step 3. If unauthorized: revoke the permission immediately, audit sent items in both the mailbox owner's mailbox and the grantee's account for any messages sent in the past 24 hours that may have been impersonating the owner.

Step 4. For high-value mailbox owners (executives, finance): the audit should go further — check whether any external recipients of mail in the past day reported receiving suspicious requests. BEC fraud sometimes propagates outward.

Step 5. Review the customer's SendAs grant pattern. SendAs on executive mailboxes should be rare, documented, and reviewed regularly.
ual_send_as_sendonbehalf_permission_gran.example_scenario:
At 08:15 on a Monday, the customer's Exchange Admin grants SendAs on the CEO's mailbox to the controller's account. Both accounts (Exchange Admin and controller) are listed as compromised by an earlier alert in the same morning.

This is BEC setup. The attacker has compromised both accounts via earlier credential theft, granted SendAs to the controller's account so the controller's account can send mail "as" the CEO, and is preparing to send a wire-transfer fraud request from the CEO to the CFO. The mail will look indistinguishable from a legitimate CEO email.

You'd: revoke the SendAs grant immediately, audit sent items in both the controller's mailbox and the CEO's mailbox for any outbound mail in the past hour, reset all three accounts (Exchange Admin, controller, anyone else flagged), revoke sessions, force phishing-resistant MFA, notify the CFO and the customer's leadership directly out-of-band that BEC was attempted (so they can verify any pending wire instructions), and search the customer's broader admin account list for other compromises.

conditional_access_policy_changed.what_is_this: A Conditional Access policy in the customer's tenant was created, modified, or deleted. CA policies are the security guardrails that decide who can sign in, from where, on what device, and under what conditions (MFA required, compliant device required, blocked country, etc.). Any change to a CA policy is potentially a critical security configuration change.
conditional_access_policy_changed.why_it_matters: CA policies are the customer's primary defense against credential attacks. They enforce MFA, restrict access to managed devices, block sign-ins from unsafe locations, and protect privileged accounts. An attacker who can modify CA policies can disable protections — turn off MFA enforcement, add their own IP to an allow list, exempt themselves from device compliance, or just delete the policy entirely. Tracking every CA change is non-negotiable; this alert exists because CA tampering is one of the highest-impact post-compromise actions an attacker can take.
conditional_access_policy_changed.attack_vectors:
What an unauthorized CA change looks like:

- A protection-weakening modification. The policy now applies to fewer users, or excludes a group the attacker controls, or removes the MFA requirement.

- A policy deletion. The whole gate is gone.

- A new policy that exempts specific users or IPs. The attacker creates a permissive policy that gives their controlled account a backdoor.

- Legitimate IT change. The customer's IT or MSP is tuning policies. Routine, but should still be documented and verified.
conditional_access_policy_changed.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the policy, the type of change (created, modified, deleted), the operator, the source IP, and the time.

Step 2. Verify with the operator out-of-band that the change is intentional. Verify with the customer's documented change process — did this go through a change request?

Step 3. Look at the diff if available. Panoptica365 may have the before/after policy state. The change details tell you whether protection was weakened or strengthened.

Step 4. If unauthorized: revert the policy to its previous state, treat the operator's account as compromised, audit recent activity, escalate.

Step 5. Even if authorized, review whether the change is appropriate. Loosening CA policies is occasionally necessary but always worth a second pair of eyes.
conditional_access_policy_changed.example_scenario:
At 04:32 on a Saturday, the customer's Global Admin modifies a CA policy named "Require MFA for all users." The change adds a new excluded group called "MFA Exception Group" to the policy. The GA is asleep in Calgary.

This is post-compromise CA tampering. The attacker has the GA's credentials, created a new group called "MFA Exception Group" earlier, added their own controlled accounts to that group, and is now excluding that group from MFA enforcement so they can sign in with just a password — bypassing the customer's primary defense.

You'd: revert the CA policy change immediately (remove the excluded group), delete the suspicious exception group and remove its members, reset the GA's password and revoke all sessions, force MFA re-registration on phishing-resistant method, audit the GA's actions in the past few hours for any other CA changes, app registrations, role grants, or MFA tampering, notify the customer's leadership and engage IR support.

ca_policy_drift_detected.what_is_this: A Conditional Access policy in the customer's tenant has drifted from its expected template configuration. "Drift" means the live policy in Microsoft no longer matches the template Panoptica365 has on file for that policy — settings have been changed, conditions adjusted, or the policy was modified outside Panoptica365's tracking. This is detected by snapshot-comparison: Panoptica365 polls the live state and compares to the stored template.
ca_policy_drift_detected.why_it_matters: Drift detection is how an MSP keeps a fleet of customer tenants consistent. The promise of Panoptica365 is that all customer tenants get the same baseline of security policies — drift means a specific tenant has fallen out of that baseline. Either someone modified the policy intentionally for a customer-specific reason (which should be documented), or someone modified it without authorization (compromise, ad-hoc fix, accidental change). Both scenarios deserve attention; the difference is what you do about it.
ca_policy_drift_detected.attack_vectors:
Drift can come from:

- Operator change without documentation. An MSP technician adjusted the policy for a customer-specific need but didn't update the template. Common and benign-ish, but breaks fleet consistency.

- Customer-side IT change. The customer's own IT (when they have one) changed the policy directly in Microsoft. Less common in MSP-managed tenants but happens.

- Active intrusion. An attacker has compromised an admin and is modifying CA policies to weaken protection.

- Microsoft platform change. Microsoft renamed a setting, changed a default, or migrated a feature, and the snapshot now appears different even though no operator changed anything.
ca_policy_drift_detected.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the alert and review the diff between the template and the live policy. Panoptica365 shows what specifically drifted.

Step 2. Identify whether the drift weakens or strengthens protection. A drift that adds users to MFA enforcement is benign; a drift that removes users is concerning.

Step 3. Check the customer change log for the relevant time window. If an operator made the change and logged it, attribution closes the loop.

Step 4. If the drift is unauthorized and weakens protection: remediate (push the template back to the live policy via Panoptica365), treat as potential compromise, audit recent admin activity.

Step 5. If the drift is intentional and customer-specific: update the template (or branch a customer-specific template) so future polls don't keep alerting.
ca_policy_drift_detected.example_scenario:
Panoptica365 detects that the customer's "Block legacy authentication" CA policy now has the user "servicedesk@customer.com" in its exclusion list. The template doesn't include that exclusion.

You check the customer's change log: nothing recorded. You check who made the change in the audit log: a junior MSP technician's account. You contact the technician — they say they added the exclusion last week because the customer's old IMAP-based scanner couldn't authenticate without legacy auth, and they didn't have time to set up a proper service principal.

This is benign drift but ugly long-term. The exclusion should be replaced with a proper modern-auth solution (or the scanner should be replaced), and the template should be updated to reflect the customer-specific exception until then. You'd: document the exception in the customer's change log, either accept the drift as intentional (update template) or schedule the fix to remove the exclusion, and brief the technician on the proper documentation process.

ca_policy_drift_remediated.what_is_this: A drifted CA policy was automatically remediated back to its template configuration. Panoptica365 detected drift, the policy was set to remediate-mode, and the live policy was overwritten with the template's settings. This alert is the positive companion of "CA Policy Drift Detected" — it confirms the drift was undone.
ca_policy_drift_remediated.why_it_matters: Auto-remediation closes the loop on drift detection. Without it, an operator would have to manually push the template back every time something drifts. The alert exists so operators have visibility into what was changed back automatically — auto-remediation is a powerful tool but it can mask intentional changes if a customer-specific drift was supposed to stay. Reading these alerts tells you whether the auto-fix is doing the right thing.
ca_policy_drift_remediated.attack_vectors:
Why this alert matters even though the action is positive:

- Persistence by an attacker. An attacker keeps modifying a CA policy to weaken it. Panoptica365 keeps remediating it. The remediation alerts spike in volume — that's your signal there's a fight happening.

- Customer-specific drift that should have been kept. The drift was intentional and the auto-remediator undid it. The customer is now back to the template state and the original need (which prompted the drift) is unmet.

- A misconfigured remediate-mode setting. A policy was set to remediate but shouldn't have been; now it's overwriting changes operators want.
ca_policy_drift_remediated.what_to_do:
Step 1. Read the alert and confirm what was remediated. Panoptica365 shows the diff that was undone.

Step 2. Check whether the drift was authorized. If yes, the remediation may have undone a legitimate customer-specific change — either re-apply the change as a template branch, or move the policy out of remediate mode.

Step 3. Check whether the drift is recurring. Repeated remediations on the same policy mean someone (or something) keeps making the same change. Investigate the source.

Step 4. If the remediation looks normal (one-off cleanup of an accidental change): no action needed.

Step 5. Periodically review the auto-remediation behavior across the fleet — make sure it's catching real drift and not fighting with intentional customer customizations.
ca_policy_drift_remediated.example_scenario:
Over a 24-hour period, you see five CA-Policy-Drift-Remediated alerts on the customer's "Require MFA for admins" policy. Each remediation undoes the same change: removing the customer's Global Admin from the policy's scope. The MSP's template requires the GA to be in scope (with no exemption).

This is repeated tampering. Someone — or some process — keeps trying to exempt the GA from MFA. You investigate: the GA's account is being used to make the change each time. The attacker has the GA's credentials, is repeatedly modifying the policy, and Panoptica365's remediator is repeatedly undoing the change. The remediator has been doing its job.

You'd: reset the GA's password and revoke all sessions immediately, force MFA re-registration on phishing-resistant method, audit what else the GA's account has been doing in the past 24 hours, escalate as critical incident, and consider whether the customer needs broader IR engagement. Without Panoptica365's remediator, the attacker would have succeeded in disabling MFA on the GA on the first try.

ca_exemption_list_changed.what_is_this: A CA policy's exemption list (excludeUsers or excludeGroups) was modified outside Panoptica365. The exemption list is the set of users/groups that the policy does NOT apply to — so changes here can dramatically alter who's protected. This is informational because the change might be legitimate (a customer-specific exception) but it warrants attention because exemptions are a common privilege-escalation path.
ca_exemption_list_changed.why_it_matters: Exemptions are how an attacker quietly disables CA enforcement for specific users. Rather than deleting a policy or modifying its scope (loud, easily noticed), the attacker just adds their controlled user to the policy's exclude list. The policy still exists, the policy still appears active in any compliance scan, but the protected pool no longer includes the attacker's account. This is the stealth weakening pattern.
ca_exemption_list_changed.attack_vectors:
What an unauthorized exemption-list change typically indicates:

- Stealth weakening. Attacker adds their controlled account to an exclusion list to bypass the policy. Hard to spot in an audit because the policy looks intact.

- Legitimate customer exception. A specific user needs the policy bypassed for a particular workflow.

- Operational change without documentation. Common in MSP environments where exclusion management happens ad-hoc.
ca_exemption_list_changed.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the policy, what was added or removed from the exemption list, and the operator.

Step 2. If users were added to the exclusion list: investigate each one. Why does this user need to bypass this policy? Were they added by an authorized operator?

Step 3. If users were removed from the exclusion list: protection just expanded. Less concerning but still worth knowing about.

Step 4. Verify with the operator out-of-band.

Step 5. If unauthorized: revert the change, treat operator account as compromised, audit recent activity.
ca_exemption_list_changed.example_scenario:
On a Monday morning, an exemption is added to the customer's "Block legacy auth" CA policy. The added user is a sales account that normally goes through Modern Auth without issue. The operator who made the change is the customer's GA.

You contact the GA: they didn't make the change. The audit log confirms it came from an IP they don't recognize.

This is post-compromise stealth weakening. The attacker has the GA's credentials, is using them to add a sales user (whose account they've also compromised) to the legacy-auth policy's exclusion list. With legacy auth open, the attacker can use the sales account from older protocols (IMAP, POP) that often have weaker MFA enforcement.

You'd: remove the exemption, reset the GA and the sales user, revoke sessions on both, force phishing-resistant MFA, audit what the sales account has accessed via legacy protocols recently, and audit other CA-policy changes the GA has made recently for similar tampering.

intune_policy_drift_detected.what_is_this: An Intune policy in the customer's tenant has drifted from its expected template configuration. Same conceptual mechanism as CA Policy Drift but for Intune's device/app configuration policies — what BitLocker settings devices need, what Windows update policies are pushed, what apps are required, what compliance settings apply. Detected via snapshot comparison.
intune_policy_drift_detected.why_it_matters: Intune policies enforce the security baseline on managed devices: encryption, password requirements, OS updates, app blocklists, conditional-access compliance signals. Drift in an Intune policy means the customer's devices may be falling out of the customer's security baseline. A drifted compliance policy could mean devices that previously qualified as "compliant" no longer do (or worse, that devices that shouldn't qualify now do). For tenants where CA depends on device compliance, drift here breaks the whole CA gate.
intune_policy_drift_detected.attack_vectors:
Drift typically comes from:

- Intentional change without template update. Same as CA — operator made a customer-specific change but didn't propagate it to the template.

- Microsoft platform change. Intune updates frequently and sometimes settings rename or change defaults; drift may be a snapshot-only artifact.

- Active intrusion. Less common than CA tampering because Intune changes require Intune Admin role specifically, but possible.

- Customer-side IT change. The customer's IT modified policies directly in Intune.
intune_policy_drift_detected.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the alert and review the diff. Panoptica365 shows what specifically changed.

Step 2. Identify whether the drift weakens device security (e.g., BitLocker requirement loosened, password length reduced, OS update enforcement disabled).

Step 3. Check the audit log for who made the change.

Step 4. If the drift is unauthorized and weakens security: remediate via Panoptica365, treat operator account as compromised, audit.

Step 5. If the drift is intentional but customer-specific: update the template branch for that customer.
intune_policy_drift_detected.example_scenario:
Panoptica365 detects that the customer's BitLocker policy now has "Require BitLocker on system drive" set to Not Configured. The template requires it to be Required. The change came from the customer's IT helper account.

You check the customer change log: nothing recorded. You contact the IT helper out-of-band: they didn't make the change. The audit log shows the change came from an IP in a foreign country.

This is post-compromise device-security weakening. The attacker has the IT helper's credentials and is loosening BitLocker so any device they later compromise can have its disk imaged or its data extracted offline. Future attacks on the customer's devices will succeed more easily.

You'd: remediate the Intune policy back to template via Panoptica365 immediately, reset the IT helper's password and revoke sessions, force MFA re-registration on phishing-resistant method, audit what other Intune changes the helper made recently, and check whether any of the customer's devices have actually drifted out of compliance under the loosened policy in the past hour.

security_setting_drift_detected.what_is_this: A security setting under Panoptica365's baseline has drifted from its applied or matched value. "Security settings" here means tenant-level security configuration tracked by Panoptica365's Security Settings Engine — things like Block Legacy Authentication, External Forwarding default, SafeLinks settings, MFA registration policies, etc. Fired when a slow-tier poll detects current state no longer matches the captured baseline. Severity is computed per-alert from the setting's priority (critical → severe, high → high, etc.).
security_setting_drift_detected.why_it_matters: Security settings are the tenant's static security posture — the configuration that doesn't change often but matters enormously when it does. Many security settings have no Conditional-Access-style detection because they're just on/off knobs in a console. The Security Settings Engine is how Panoptica365 notices when those knobs get flipped, intentionally or otherwise. Drift here means the customer is now operating outside the security posture that was previously approved.
security_setting_drift_detected.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- An attacker has compromised an admin and is loosening a setting to enable later attacks (e.g., re-enabling Legacy Auth, disabling Modern Auth, enabling tenant-wide auto-forwarding).

- Routine admin change without documentation. Operator made a deliberate change, didn't log it, the drift detector caught up.

- Microsoft platform change. Microsoft updated a default value or renamed a property; the snapshot now appears different.

- Customer-side change in the Microsoft admin console.
security_setting_drift_detected.what_to_do:
Step 1. Open the alert and read which setting drifted, the prior value, and the new value. Panoptica365's drift detector shows both.

Step 2. Note the severity. Critical-priority settings (e.g., Block Legacy Auth, Disable Tenant-Wide Forwarding) are severe alerts; minor settings are lower priority.

Step 3. Check whether the change weakens or tightens security. Tightening drift is benign (and sometimes welcome); weakening drift is what to focus on.

Step 4. Identify the operator from the audit log. Verify out-of-band.

Step 5. If unauthorized: remediate via Panoptica365 (push baseline back), treat operator as compromised, audit, escalate.

Step 6. If intentional: accept the drift in Panoptica365 (which updates the baseline) so future polls don't keep firing.
security_setting_drift_detected.example_scenario:
Panoptica365 fires a severe-severity drift alert: the tenant-wide "Disable Auto-Forwarding" setting in EOP has changed from Enabled to Disabled. The customer's GA made the change overnight.

This is post-compromise mail-exfiltration setup. With auto-forwarding now allowed tenant-wide, any compromised user can have their mail silently forwarded to an external address — and the attacker has likely already started doing that on multiple users. The disable was the gate; now the gate is open.

You'd: remediate the setting back to Disabled via Panoptica365 immediately, reset the GA's password and revoke sessions, force phishing-resistant MFA re-registration, audit Set-Mailbox forwarding additions across the tenant since the change (likely yes), audit inbox-rule creations across users since the change, search for any external addresses receiving mass forwards, escalate as critical incident, and notify customer leadership about potential mail data exfiltration.

ual_transport_rule_changed.what_is_this: A tenant-wide Exchange transport rule (mail flow rule) was created, modified, removed, enabled, or disabled. Transport rules apply to all mail flowing through the tenant — they can route messages, add disclaimers, block content, redirect mail to external addresses, or copy mail to other mailboxes. Severe when the rule has BlindCopyTo or RedirectMessageTo (mail-exfiltration vectors) set.
ual_transport_rule_changed.why_it_matters: Transport rules operate at the tenant level on every email — inbound, outbound, internal. A malicious transport rule is one of the cleanest mail-exfiltration techniques in Microsoft 365 because it's invisible to individual users (no inbox rule for them to see), it operates on all mail (not just one mailbox), and the only place to detect it is the audit log. Attackers love transport rules because they survive password resets, MFA changes, and most incident response actions that focus on user accounts.
ual_transport_rule_changed.attack_vectors:
What this typically indicates:

- Tenant-wide mail exfiltration. Attacker creates a transport rule that BCCs all mail (or all mail matching a filter) to an external address they control.

- Selective targeting. A rule that copies mail involving specific keywords ("wire," "invoice," "contract") or specific senders/recipients (executives, finance) to an external destination.

- Disabling existing protective rules. The customer has a transport rule that strips dangerous attachments or quarantines suspicious senders, and the attacker disabled it to let their own follow-up attacks through.

- Legitimate IT change. Adding a disclaimer, fixing routing, adjusting compliance.
ual_transport_rule_changed.what_to_do:
Step 1. Identify the rule, what changed (created/modified/disabled/etc.), the operator, the source IP, and the time.

Step 2. If the rule has BlindCopyTo or RedirectMessageTo set: critical. The rule is exfiltrating mail. Disable it immediately while you investigate.

Step 3. Verify with the operator out-of-band. Transport rules should always have a documented business justification.

Step 4. If unauthorized: disable the rule, audit Exchange's mail tracking logs to identify any mail that was actually copied or redirected, notify the customer's leadership about potential data exfiltration, treat the operator's account as compromised.

Step 5. Review the customer's full transport-rule list. Other malicious or stale rules may be sitting alongside.
ual_transport_rule_changed.example_scenario:
At 02:45 on a Sunday, a new transport rule named "Compliance Forwarding" is created in the customer's tenant. The rule's logic: BCC every email containing the words "wire" OR "invoice" OR "payment" to an external address at protonmail.com. The operator is the customer's Exchange Admin, who is asleep.

This is post-compromise mail exfiltration via transport rule. The attacker has the Exchange Admin's credentials, created a rule that exfiltrates every financial email from the tenant to their controlled inbox. The rule is invisible to users (no inbox rule), runs on all mail (not just one mailbox), and will keep working until someone notices.
