_comment_security_settings: Per-setting registry strings keyed by setting_id (EXO-01, ENT-05, CMP-01, etc.). Lookup pattern: security_settings.<setting_id>.<field> where field ∈ {name, security_impact, user_impact, admin_notes}. Missing key → JS falls back to canonical English from src/lib/security-settings/registry.js. Add new settings here as the registry grows; reciprocal entries belong in fr.json/es.json/etc.

EXO-01.name: Enable MailTips (All Tips + External Recipients)
EXO-01.security_impact: Most accidental data leaks in small businesses happen when someone sends an email to the wrong recipient — a client with the same first name, an old distribution list, the wrong Bob. MailTips puts a visible warning above the Send button so the user sees the mistake before the mail leaves the tenant.
EXO-01.user_impact: Users will see a small banner above their email draft when they are sending to someone outside the organization, to a large group, or to a mailbox with unusual settings. No extra clicks, no blocking — just an advisory line.
EXO-01.admin_notes: Safe to enable in any tenant. The external-recipient tip is off by default in most tenants, which is the single most valuable flag to flip. The large-audience threshold is set to 25 by Apply, matching the CIS recommendation; tune manually via PowerShell for tenants with very large distribution lists.
EXO-01.writer_recommended_label: The "Enabled" preset turns on all three MailTips features (general tips, external-recipient warnings, large-audience warnings) and sets the large-audience threshold to 25 — matching CIS guidance.

interpreted.external_on_others_partial: External warnings ON, other tips partial
interpreted.external_disabled: External recipient warnings DISABLED — risk (most common SMB default)
interpreted.fully_off: MailTips fully OFF — no sender-side warnings

EXO-02.name: Disable Automatic Forwarding to External Domains
EXO-02.security_impact: When an attacker compromises an account, the first thing they usually do is create a silent inbox rule that forwards every new message to an address they control. Disabling auto-forward at the transport layer blocks this exfiltration path even if the inbox rule still exists.
EXO-02.user_impact: Users will no longer be able to set up automatic forwarding to a personal email (e.g. Gmail, Hotmail). Forwarding to other internal colleagues still works. If any user legitimately forwards to a partner company, that exception needs to be documented and handled via a dedicated remote domain, not the Default one.
EXO-02.admin_notes: Before applying, enumerate existing inbox rules with `Get-InboxRule -ResultSize Unlimited | Where {$_.ForwardTo}` to identify users who currently rely on external forwarding. This setting only modifies the Default remote domain; non-Default remote domains (e.g. a partner federation stub) are left alone because they almost always represent a deliberate exception.
EXO-02.writer_recommended_label: Disabling auto-forward on the Default remote domain blocks the most common BEC exfiltration vector. Non-Default remote domains (deliberate partner federations) are unaffected.

writer_options.auto_forward_enabled_on_default_domain_b: Auto-forward enabled on Default domain (BEC exfiltration vector)

disabled_with_others.one: Disabled on Default ({count} other remote domain — review manually)
disabled_with_others.other: Disabled on Default ({count} other remote domains — review manually)

interpreted.enabled: ENABLED on Default — risk (BEC exfiltration vector)

enabled_with_others.one: ENABLED on Default — risk ({count} other remote domain)
enabled_with_others.other: ENABLED on Default — risk ({count} other remote domains)

EXO-03.name: Enable Mailbox Auditing for All Users
EXO-03.security_impact: If an account is compromised, there is no way to investigate what the attacker did without mailbox audit logs. Owner-action logging is what captures an attacker who is already logged in as the user. This setting is the prerequisite for every email-based forensic investigation.
EXO-03.user_impact: Users will not notice any change. Auditing runs silently in the background and has no impact on mailbox performance or behaviour.
EXO-03.admin_notes: Microsoft enabled mailbox auditing by default in 2019, but owner-action logging is still narrower than the recommended set. Apply pre-filters Get-Mailbox to only the mailboxes that need updating (those currently with AuditEnabled = $false), so on a typical SMB tenant where most mailboxes are already enabled, Apply runs quickly. On tenants with many disabled mailboxes (post-migration scenarios are common), Apply may take several minutes — the busy panel's elapsed counter will tick past the usual 5-15 second window. Writer timeout bumped to 5 min to accommodate this. No user-visible impact (audit runs silently in the background).
EXO-03.writer_recommended_label: Mailbox auditing is the prerequisite for every email-based forensic investigation. Microsoft enables it by default on new mailboxes since 2019 and enforces audit tenant-wide on modern tenants — choosing "Disabled" here only takes effect on pre-2019 tenants where the per-mailbox flag was explicitly set to False. Apply syncs every user mailbox to AuditEnabled = $true.

writer_options.disabled_per_mailbox_flag_off_no_effect_: Disabled — per-mailbox flag off (no effect on modern tenants)

all_enabled.one: Auditing enabled on all {total} user mailbox (recommended)
all_enabled.other: Auditing enabled on all {total} user mailboxes (recommended)

interpreted.partial_gap: Auditing DISABLED on {disabled} of {total} mailboxes — gap

EXO-05.name: Configure Anti-Spam Outbound Policy (Restrict Compromised Accounts)
EXO-05.security_impact: Compromised accounts are used to spray phishing and spam to partners and clients. Left unchecked, this gets the entire tenant domain flagged on reputation blacklists — recovery is painful. Automatic restriction contains the blast radius in minutes and signals the MSP before any real damage lands.
EXO-05.user_impact: Users will not notice any change unless their account starts sending spam — in which case the account is blocked from sending any outbound email until the MSP investigates and releases it.
EXO-05.admin_notes: Set the notification recipient to an MSP-monitored address that someone actually reads (the MSP security inbox or ticketing queue). If the notification goes into a black hole, the value of this setting is halved. Apply uses BlockUserForToday (24h auto-release) — operators can manually escalate to permanent BlockUser via the portal if a real compromise is confirmed.
EXO-05.writer_recommended_label: The "Block + alert MSP" preset stops compromised accounts from sending more outbound mail (24h auto-release) AND alerts the MSP recipient list so operators can investigate. Microsoft default is alert-only, which does NOT block the spam.

writer_options.block_compromised_accounts_24h_alert_msp: Block compromised accounts (24h) + alert MSP recipients (recommended)
writer_options.microsoft_default_alert_only_does_not_bl: Microsoft default (Alert-only — does NOT block compromised accounts)

interpreted.no_alert: {action} set but NO alerting — operator won't know when triggered
interpreted.alert_only: Alert-only — does NOT block compromised accounts (default — risk)

EXO-06.name: Enable Preset Security Policy (Standard or Strict) — MDO
EXO-06.security_impact: Default Exchange settings leak a lot of phishing. Microsoft's Strict preset adds zero-hour auto-purge, aggressive phishing verdicts, and impersonation protection the default policy doesn't have. Using the preset means the MSP gets Microsoft's expert tuning for free, updated automatically — no need to hand-maintain a custom policy per tenant.
EXO-06.user_impact: Users will see more suspected-phishing messages quarantined and more file attachments blocked or delayed for sandbox analysis. False-positive rate is higher on Strict than Standard; a handful of legitimate emails may land in quarantine the first week and need release.
EXO-06.admin_notes: Standard is the recommended baseline for every tenant — Microsoft's own default recommendation. Strict adds aggressive impersonation protection and stricter quarantine on top of Standard, scoped to high-risk users (executives, finance, anyone notorious for clicking on anything). PREREQUISITE: the preset RULES must exist on the tenant before Apply can flip them. Microsoft auto-creates them the first time someone visits the Defender portal Preset Security Policies page (security.microsoft.com → Email & collaboration → Policies & rules → Threat policies → Preset Security Policies) and walks through the wizard. Apply throws a clear error if the rules are missing. (Auto-creation deferred to a future version — Microsoft's New-EOPProtectionPolicyRule cmdlet requires three separate policy lookups per tier, plus the actual policy names contain timestamp suffixes that vary per tenant, so we want to test that path on a fresh tenant before shipping.) Apply preserves rule scope across runs — only State flips. Drift detection covers rule state AND impersonation lists (TargetedUsersToProtect, TargetedDomainsToProtect, ExcludedDomains) on the underlying anti-phish policy.
EXO-06.writer_recommended_label: Standard alone is the floor every tenant should have — turning it on tenant-wide is the single highest-value EXO hardening step. Standard + Strict adds defense in depth for tenants with high-value targets (executives, finance, legal). Disabled is "default Microsoft policies" which Microsoft acknowledges is loose.

writer_options.standard_strict_defense_in_depth_strict_: Standard + Strict (defense in depth — Strict for high-risk users on top of Standard baseline)
writer_options.no_presets_tenant_runs_on_default_polici: No presets — tenant runs on default policies (Microsoft acknowledges these are loose)

interpreted.strict_only: Strict preset enabled (recommended for high-risk tenants)
interpreted.rules_disabled: Preset rules defined but DISABLED — running on default policies
interpreted.no_preset: No preset configured — running on default policies (loose)

EXO-08.name: Disable Basic Auth for SMTP AUTH Submission
EXO-08.security_impact: SMTP AUTH uses basic username/password authentication, which bypasses MFA entirely. Attackers running credential-stuffing campaigns target SMTP AUTH because a single correct password gets them into the mailbox with no MFA prompt. Disabling it globally closes a major bypass.
EXO-08.user_impact: Users will not notice any change on modern Outlook or Outlook on the Web — those use modern auth. Impact is only on legacy scanners, printers, and line-of-business apps that send email directly via SMTP AUTH. Those need to be identified, migrated to Graph-based sending, or exempted explicitly.
EXO-08.admin_notes: Before applying, audit the tenant's scanners, multi-function printers, and any in-house app that sends email. For each still on SMTP AUTH, plan a migration to Graph sendMail or to an SMTP relay that uses app passwords with a dedicated service account.
EXO-08.writer_recommended_label: Setting this to "Disabled" turns off basic-auth SMTP submission tenant-wide. Per-mailbox overrides are still possible for specific service accounts that genuinely need SMTP AUTH.

writer_options.smtp_auth_enabled_tenant_wide_basic_auth: SMTP AUTH enabled tenant-wide (basic-auth credential-stuffing exposure)

interpreted.enabled: SMTP AUTH ENABLED tenant-wide — risk (basic-auth credential-stuffing exposure)
interpreted.default: Using Microsoft default — verify in EAC (current default = disabled)

ENT-01.name: Enable Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) for All Users
ENT-01.security_impact: SSPR drives users to register multiple authentication methods, which directly improves MFA coverage and account recovery. It also eliminates helpdesk password-reset workflows, which are a favourite social-engineering target — an attacker who can call the helpdesk and impersonate a user often walks away with a reset password.
ENT-01.user_impact: Users will be prompted to register authentication methods the next time they sign in. After that, they can reset their own password at aka.ms/sspr without involving the helpdesk.
ENT-01.admin_notes: Requires Entra ID P1, which is included in Business Premium. Scope should normally be all users. Microsoft has migrated SSPR enablement into the Authentication Methods Policy — there is no longer a single "SSPR on/off" toggle exposed via Graph. Instead: if the right authentication methods are enabled with the right user targets, SSPR works. Apply enables Microsoft Authenticator + SMS + Email auth methods with includeTarget=all_users — the Microsoft-recommended SSPR baseline. Operators wanting more methods (FIDO2, Voice, OATH tokens) configure those manually via the Entra portal; Apply preserves them since each method is a separate sub-resource.
ENT-01.writer_recommended_label: The "Standard SSPR config" enables Microsoft Authenticator (preferred), SMS (fallback), and Email (recovery) for all users — Microsoft's recommended SSPR baseline. Optional advanced methods (FIDO2, TAP, OATH tokens, etc.) can be enabled via the "Additional Authentication Methods" expandable section below.

writer_options.standard_sspr_config_authenticator_sms_e: Standard SSPR config — Authenticator + SMS + Email enabled for all users (recommended)

secondary_section.help: Optional methods to enable on top of the SSPR baseline. Useful for tenants with passwordless rollouts, hardware tokens, or first-time-access flows. Methods you don't check will be DISABLED on Apply — Panoptica365 syncs the complete set so drift detection catches any external change.

options.TemporaryAccessPass: Temporary Access Pass (first-time setup, account recovery)
options.Voice: Voice Call (legacy fallback for accounts without phones)
options.QRCodePin: QR Code + PIN (frontline worker scenarios, preview)

interpreted.none_enabled: No auth methods enabled — SSPR cannot function

methods_enabled.one: {count} auth method enabled — SSPR scope not exposed via Graph
methods_enabled.other: {count} auth methods enabled — SSPR scope not exposed via Graph

ENT-05.name: Enable Number Matching and Additional Context for MFA Push
ENT-05.security_impact: MFA fatigue attacks (also called prompt bombing) work by spamming the user with push notifications until they tap Approve by accident. Number matching breaks this: the user has to see the sign-in screen to know the number, so they cannot approve on reflex. This is one of the highest-ROI MFA hardening steps available.
ENT-05.user_impact: Users will see a two-digit number on the sign-in screen and be asked to type it into the Authenticator app instead of just tapping Approve. The app will also show which application they are signing into and the approximate city the sign-in is coming from.
ENT-05.admin_notes: Microsoft enabled number matching for all tenants by default in May 2023 and removed the opt-out — so the only operator-controllable bits are the app-name and location displays. Apply explicitly enables both, which both improves UX clarity AND establishes a baseline so future drift (e.g. an admin experimentally disabling them) is caught.
ENT-05.writer_recommended_label: The recommended configuration enables both app-name and location-context displays alongside Microsoft's tenant-default number matching. The two extras make prompt-bombing attacks meaningfully harder by forcing the user to look at the sign-in screen.

writer_options.number_matching_app_and_location_context: Number matching + app and location context (recommended)
writer_options.number_matching_only_no_extra_context_di: Number matching only — no extra context displayed

interpreted.partial: Partial — number matching on, context partial
interpreted.tenant_default: Tenant default (number matching enforced since May 2023)

ENT-06.name: Configure Entra ID Password Protection (Banned Password List)
ENT-06.security_impact: Default complexity rules are easy to satisfy with passwords like "Company2024!" — which attackers include in their password-spray dictionaries. Banned-password enforcement rejects these predictable patterns at set-time, which is far more effective than forcing frequent rotation.
ENT-06.user_impact: Users will see a rejection when they try to set a password that contains a banned term. The error message is clear; they simply pick a different password.
ENT-06.admin_notes: The global Microsoft-maintained list is free. The custom list (tenant-specific banned terms) requires Entra ID P1. Keep the custom list short and memorable — 8-12 terms covering company name, brand names, and local geographic references. Anything longer has diminishing returns. Apply auto-creates the Password Rule Settings template on first use (POST /groupSettings) and updates it on subsequent edits (PATCH). Default lockout-threshold of 10 and lockout-duration of 60 seconds match Microsoft's recommendation; tune via portal if needed.
ENT-06.writer_recommended_label: Custom banned words catch the company-specific patterns Microsoft's global list won't — your company name, product names, local geography, founder/exec names. 8-12 terms is the sweet spot. Anything longer has diminishing returns.

writer_options.global_microsoft_list_tenant_specific_cu: Global Microsoft list + tenant-specific custom banned words (recommended)
writer_options.global_microsoft_list_only_no_tenant_spe: Global Microsoft list only (no tenant-specific words)

writer_input_help.global_plus_custom: One banned word or phrase per line, 4-16 characters each. These will be rejected when users try to use them in passwords. Microsoft's global list of common-passwords is always applied on top of this.

interpreted.default: Default (Microsoft global banned-list only; no custom list)

global_plus_custom.one: Global + custom list ({count} banned term, lockout={lockout})
global_plus_custom.other: Global + custom list ({count} banned terms, lockout={lockout})

interpreted.custom_empty: Custom check enabled but list is empty (lockout={lockout})

ENT-07.name: Restrict User Consent for Third-Party Applications
ENT-07.security_impact: OAuth consent phishing sends a user an innocent-looking link that ends up on a Microsoft consent screen asking for mail-read or file-read permission. Users routinely approve without reading. Restricting user consent forces every new app grant through an admin review queue — stopping this attack vector cold.
ENT-07.user_impact: Users will no longer be able to approve third-party app permission requests on their own. If they encounter a legitimate app that needs consent, they will see a message asking them to request admin approval. The MSP receives these requests and approves them case-by-case.
ENT-07.admin_notes: If choosing "Do not allow user consent", set up the admin consent request workflow first — otherwise legitimate app requests get stuck with nowhere to go. Configure the request review recipient to an MSP address that is actually monitored. The doc page at aka.ms/adminconsentrequests walks through the setup. The "Microsoft-managed" option is the safest baseline for tenants without an admin-consent workflow yet.
ENT-07.writer_recommended_label: Microsoft now recommends "Let Microsoft manage your consent settings" — they auto-update the underlying policy as their guidance evolves. Choose "Do not allow user consent" only if you have an admin-consent request workflow set up and monitored.

writer_options.let_microsoft_manage_your_consent_settin: Let Microsoft manage your consent settings (recommended)
writer_options.do_not_allow_user_consent_admin_consent_: Do not allow user consent (admin-consent workflow required)
writer_options.allow_user_consent_for_verified_publishe: Allow user consent for verified publishers, low-impact permissions only

interpreted.open_legacy: Open (legacy — users can consent to any app)

ENT-09.name: Disable Guest User Default Permissions
ENT-09.security_impact: Default guest permissions let an invited outsider browse a surprising amount of the directory — full group list, full user list, device names. If a guest account gets compromised, that becomes a reconnaissance toolkit. The Restricted role removes this.
ENT-09.user_impact: Guest users will not see the organisation's global address list or group directory. They continue to see the specific teams, channels, and files they have been explicitly added to.
ENT-09.admin_notes: Test with a sample guest account in each tenant before widespread rollout — a small percentage of tenants rely on guests being able to browse membership, and the change will need to be explained. Low rate of legitimate impact; high reconnaissance-prevention value.
ENT-09.writer_recommended_label: Microsoft recommends Restricted Guest User for tenants that do not need guests browsing the directory.

SPO-01.name: Restrict External Sharing (Limit to Existing Guests or Disable)
SPO-01.security_impact: "Anyone" links give unauthenticated access to whoever has the URL — the link can be forwarded, leaked, or indexed, with no audit trail of who actually read the file. Restricting to authenticated sharing (or disabling external entirely) means every access is tied to a named identity and is loggable.
SPO-01.user_impact: Users will see fewer sharing options in the Share dialog. "Anyone with the link" will be removed or limited to specific scoped cases. Sharing with existing guests and internal colleagues continues to work normally.
SPO-01.admin_notes: Most SMB tenants can safely drop to "Existing guests only" without business impact. Before applying, run an audit of active anonymous links — existing links are not retroactively invalidated, but newly-created ones will follow the new policy. The full rollback path is trivial: a single PowerShell call to restore the prior setting.
SPO-01.writer_recommended_label: Microsoft recommends "Existing guests only" for SMB tenants that share with named partners but never with anonymous users.

SPO-02.name: Restrict OneDrive Sync App on Unmanaged Devices
SPO-02.security_impact: Without this restriction, a user signed in on a personal computer can install the OneDrive sync client and pull every shared file onto their unmanaged device. Once synced, the MSP has no visibility or recourse — the data is on a machine outside the security perimeter. Restricting the sync app stops this specific exfiltration path.
SPO-02.user_impact: Users will see the OneDrive sync client refuse to run on their personal computers. Web-based access (reading files in the browser) is unaffected by this setting alone. On Entra-joined devices, nothing changes.
SPO-02.admin_notes: This setting only restricts the sync app. To also block web-based downloads from unmanaged devices, pair this with a Conditional Access policy that targets the SharePoint cloud app and requires a compliant device — a separate setting that lives in CA Policies. Both controls are needed for full coverage; this one is the safer half of the pair to ship first.
SPO-02.writer_recommended_label: Microsoft recommends restricting the OneDrive sync app on unmanaged devices for any tenant that has Conditional Access available.

interpreted.restricted: OneDrive sync app restricted on unmanaged devices (CA-based web-access policy not visible via Graph — verify in SPO admin centre)
interpreted.unrestricted: OneDrive sync app NOT restricted on unmanaged devices — risk (CA-based web-access policy not visible via Graph either)

TEA-01.name: Anonymous Meeting Join and Lobby Policy
TEA-01.security_impact: Anonymous join means anyone with the meeting link can enter with no authentication — no name, no audit trail. Combined with auto-admit-everyone, this is the configuration that enabled "Zoom-bombing" disruptions and corporate-meeting eavesdropping. The right answer depends on the tenant's business model: tenants whose external meeting attendees are always business contacts via federation should block anonymous; tenants running training, coaching, or sales meetings with non-business external customers need anonymous-allowed but lobby-held so the organiser controls admission.
TEA-01.user_impact: Hardened mode: external participants must sign in with a Microsoft account (any tenant, including personal). Permissive-with-lobby mode: external participants click the link and wait in the lobby until the organiser admits them — works for Google, Yahoo, or any email provider. Open mode: external participants walk straight in without any control.
TEA-01.admin_notes: CHOOSING THE RIGHT OPTION: Hardened (the original recommended) fits tenants whose external meeting attendees are always other M365 tenant users via federation. If your customer runs training sessions, coaching, sales calls, or any meetings with external NON-Microsoft users (e.g. a customer on Google Workspace), Hardened will block them — verified Apr 28, 2026 when Trilogiam's training attendee on Google Workspace was rejected at the verification screen. Permissive-with-lobby is the right answer for those tenants: anonymous can join (they go to lobby), organiser admits them, no Zoom-bombing because nobody auto-admits without organiser action. Open mode is dangerous and should never be the deliberate choice — flagged danger for that reason. Apply modifies the GLOBAL meeting policy; org-specific exception policies are untouched.
TEA-01.writer_recommended_label: There is NO universal recommendation — choose based on the tenant's external-meeting profile. Tenants that only meet with M365 federated partners → Hardened. Tenants running training, coaching, sales, or other meetings with non-business external attendees → Permissive with lobby. Open mode is genuinely dangerous and should never be deliberately chosen.

writer_options.open_anonymous_join_allowed_everyone_aut: Open — anonymous join allowed + everyone auto-admits (Zoom-bombing risk)

interpreted.open: Open — anonymous join allowed + everyone auto-admits ({autoAdmit}). Zoom-bombing exposure.

TEA-02.name: Restrict External Access and Guest Access in Teams
TEA-02.security_impact: Unrestricted Teams federation means any attacker with any Microsoft 365 tenant can send chat messages to your users with no prior relationship. This has been used for social-engineering attacks and to deliver malicious links bypassing email protection entirely.
TEA-02.user_impact: Users will no longer receive chats from unknown external tenants. Existing partner communications work unchanged if those domains are on the allowlist. If a new partner needs to be added, the MSP adds the domain to the allowlist — a quick operation.
TEA-02.admin_notes: Field reality (Jacques, Apr 26): most SMBs leave federation open to other M365 tenants for practicality, but should block personal Microsoft accounts (consumer chat is a phishing vector). Allowlist mode is for tenants with specific named partners and a clear "no other federation" requirement (legal, finance, healthcare). Apply REPLACES the allowlist — operators see the current list pre-populated in the text area before editing, and the Confirm banner shows added/removed domains as a diff before the write fires.
TEA-02.writer_recommended_label: Most SMBs want "Open + consumer blocked" — federation works with any M365 tenant for business comms, but personal Microsoft accounts cannot chat your users. Allowlist mode is the most secure but requires per-partner curation. Federation OFF is the most restrictive and may be disruptive.

CMP-01.name: Enable Unified Audit Log
CMP-01.security_impact: Without the Unified Audit Log, most security incidents cannot be investigated at all — there is no record of what the attacker did. Enabling the audit log is a prerequisite for every subsequent incident-response workflow, and many compliance frameworks require it outright.
CMP-01.user_impact: Users will not notice any change. Auditing is silent and has no performance impact.
CMP-01.admin_notes: Should already be enabled on new tenants by default, but older tenants sometimes have it off. Verify the log is retained long enough for the client's needs — 90 days is standard, Business Premium and above can extend further. Note that even after enabling the flag, Microsoft can take up to an hour to begin ingesting events into the log.
CMP-01.writer_recommended_label: Enable the Unified Audit Log on every tenant — without it, no security incident can be investigated. Microsoft typically enables this by default on new tenants, but older tenants sometimes need an explicit Apply.

interpreted.disabled: Unified Audit Log DISABLED — no forensic capability (Critical gap)

CMP-02.name: Monitor DLP Policy Configuration
CMP-02.security_impact: DLP weakening is a stealthy attack pattern: a compromised admin account or insider threat can disable a policy or remove a sensitive-type detector to exfiltrate data without tripping standard alerts. Drift detection on DLP catches these changes within minutes of the slow-tier poll.
CMP-02.user_impact: No user-facing impact — this setting is read-only in Panoptica365. Users continue to see DLP block/notify messages exactly as the operator configured them via the Purview portal.
CMP-02.admin_notes: AUDIT-ONLY setting. Panoptica365 does NOT create or modify DLP policies. Microsoft's DLP write cmdlets (New-DlpCompliancePolicy etc.) require Microsoft 365 E5 Compliance licensing when invoked via service-principal IPPS, which is above the Business Premium SMB target tier — verified Apr 27 on Trilogiam where New-DlpCompliancePolicy returned InvalidLicenseException despite the tenant having working DLP policies in Purview UI. Manage DLP policies via the Microsoft Purview portal (compliance.microsoft.com → Data Loss Prevention). Use Match in Panoptica365 to capture your current DLP configuration as a baseline; Panoptica365 then alerts on any drift. If the tenant has zero DLP policies, the setting shows grey (not_applied) with a note — Match still captures the empty state, so creating the first policy later will fire drift and surface as "new policy added".
CMP-02.warning_banner: This setting is READ-ONLY in Panoptica365. DLP policies must be created and modified via the Microsoft Purview portal (compliance.microsoft.com → Data Loss Prevention). Panoptica365 monitors your existing DLP configuration and alerts you to any change.
CMP-02.empty_state_note: No DLP policies are configured on this tenant. Click Match to capture this empty state as a baseline — Panoptica365 will alert you when any DLP policy is created. To create policies, use the Microsoft Purview portal.

interpreted.none: No DLP policies configured — Match captures the empty state as a baseline so any new policy fires drift

EXO-09.name: Strict Mailbox Audit Posture (Bypass + Action List)
EXO-09.security_impact: The "Enable Mailbox Auditing for All Users" setting turns mailbox auditing on, but mailbox audit can still silently produce zero events two ways: AuditBypassAssociation=True suppresses ALL logging despite AuditEnabled=True (legacy artifact on shared / resource mailboxes), and a manually-shrunk AuditOwner action list can exclude critical forensic actions like SoftDelete and HardDelete (evidence destruction) or UpdateFolderPermissions (covert delegation grants). Without this setting in place, "Enable Mailbox Auditing for All Users" can show all green while several mailboxes are entirely uninvestigatable. License caveat: MailItemsAccessed for AuditOwner is only available on Defender for Office 365 P2 / E3+ tenants and is NOT enforced by this setting.
EXO-09.user_impact: Users will not notice any change. Apply runs silently in the background and only adjusts internal audit configuration.
EXO-09.admin_notes: Apply "Enable Mailbox Auditing for All Users" first (turn on AuditEnabled), then this setting (lock down bypass + action list). Apply iterates gapped mailboxes and merges the standard supported-on-Business-Premium action set into AuditOwner. On bigger tenants Apply may take several minutes — timeout is 5 min. Operators wanting to extend the action list (e.g. add MailItemsAccessed on Defender P2 / E3+ tenants) can do so manually via Set-Mailbox; Apply only enforces the baseline and does NOT remove operator additions.
EXO-09.writer_recommended_label: This setting locks down the two silent-disable paths that "Enable Mailbox Auditing for All Users" cannot detect: per-mailbox AuditBypassAssociation flags AND missing critical actions in AuditOwner. Apply ensures every audit-enabled mailbox has AuditBypassEnabled=False and AuditOwner contains the standard set Microsoft supports on Business Premium: Create, SoftDelete, HardDelete, Update, Move, MoveToDeletedItems, UpdateFolderPermissions. MailItemsAccessed is license-gated above Business Premium and is intentionally NOT enforced here.

writer_options.permissive_accept_bypass_flags_partial_a: Permissive — accept bypass flags + partial action lists (forensics gap)

welcome.title: Welcome to Panoptica365
welcome.subtitle: Let's get your install configured.
welcome.body: This wizard walks you through 7 steps to finish configuring your Panoptica365 install. You'll need: Entra (Azure AD) app registration credentials, SMTP details, an Anthropic API key, and your Panoptica365 license activation key. Estimated time: 10-15 minutes.
welcome.lang_note: Use the language picker in the top-right to change the wizard's language at any time. Your selection will be saved as your operator preference after setup completes.

hostname.subtitle: The public hostname where Panoptica365 will be reachable.

app_reg.title: Entra App Registration
app_reg.subtitle: The longest step — ~10-15 minutes of Entra portal work.
app_reg.summary: Before you can paste credentials in the next step, you need an Entra app registration in your MSP's own tenant. The detailed instructions walk you through every click, with copy-to-clipboard buttons for every value and warnings around the easy-to-miss steps.
app_reg.skip_btn: I already have an app reg — skip to credentials
app_reg.intro: This is the longest step. Plan ~10-15 minutes. You'll need Global Admin access to your MSP's Entra tenant.
app_reg.warn_global_admin: Sign in as a Global Administrator of your MSP's own Entra tenant. Anything less and several steps will fail with permission errors.
app_reg.create_1: Go to entra.microsoft.com → Applications → App registrations → New registration.
app_reg.create_2: Name: Panoptica365 (or whatever you want; the name is operator-only and doesn't affect anything).
app_reg.create_3: Supported account types: Accounts in any organizational directory (Any Microsoft Entra ID tenant — Multitenant). This MUST be multi-tenant — your customers' tenants will grant consent against this app.
app_reg.create_4: Redirect URI: pick Web, paste this exact value:
app_reg.create_redirects_extra: After clicking Register, open Authentication in the app's left sidebar and add these two more Web redirect URIs (use Add a platform → Web if there is no Web section yet, otherwise Add URI). All three are required — the first signs you in to Panoptica365, the second lets you onboard customer tenants, and the third enables the Microsoft Teams configuration features:
app_reg.danger_single_tenant: Do NOT pick single-tenant. A single-tenant app reg cannot accept admin consent from customer tenants. If you picked the wrong type, delete the app reg and start over.
app_reg.h_grab_ids: Copy the Tenant ID and Application ID
app_reg.ids_1: On the new app's Overview page, find Directory (tenant) ID and Application (client) ID.
app_reg.ids_2: Save them somewhere temporary (Notepad, 1Password, etc.) — you'll paste them into the next wizard step.
app_reg.secret_1: In the left sidebar of your app, click Certificates & secrets → Client secrets → New client secret.
app_reg.secret_2: Description: Panoptica365 setup. Expires: pick whatever your security policy requires (24 months is typical).
app_reg.secret_4: Immediately copy the secret's Value column and save it. You'll paste it into the next wizard step.
app_reg.danger_value_vs_id: Copy the VALUE, not the Secret ID. The "Value" column shows the actual secret only once at creation time — if you leave the page without copying it, you'll have to delete and re-create the secret. The "Secret ID" is a different field that looks similar but will NOT work.
app_reg.cert_intro: Exchange Online PowerShell — which Panoptica365 uses to read ~24 of your security settings — requires a certificate (a client secret alone won't work for Exchange). Panoptica365 has generated one for you automatically. You just need to download its public half and upload it to this same Certificates & secrets page.
app_reg.cert_upload_1: Click Download certificate (.cer) above and save the file.
app_reg.cert_upload_2: In your app registration, go to Certificates & secrets → Certificates tab → Upload certificate.
app_reg.cert_upload_3: Choose the panoptica365.cer file you just downloaded, then click Add.
app_reg.cert_upload_4: After upload, the thumbprint Entra shows should match the one above.
app_reg.cert_warn_required: Don't skip this — without the uploaded certificate, all the Exchange Online security readers stay stuck at "Awaiting Infra." The Test Connection button on the next wizard step checks that this certificate is present and will tell you if it's missing.
app_reg.perms_intro: In your app's left sidebar, click API permissions → Add a permission. You'll add permissions from four different APIs. Within each API, Entra groups permissions alphabetically by category — the lists below follow that order so you can scroll down without searching.
app_reg.warn_app_vs_delegated: For each API, you'll switch between the Application permissions tab and the Delegated permissions tab. Don't mix them up — the wrong tab gives you a perm with the same name but different semantics.
app_reg.perms_org_tab_intro: You've finished Microsoft Graph. For each of the next three APIs, click Add a permission again — but this time open the APIs my organization uses tab (NOT "Microsoft APIs" — these are not Graph permissions) and search for the API by name.
app_reg.perms_graph_location: Open Add a permission → the Microsoft APIs tab → click the Microsoft Graph tile (the large one at the top).
app_reg.perms_find_by_name: On the APIs my organization uses tab, type {api} in the search box — or paste App ID {appId} if the name doesn't appear — then click it.
app_reg.perms_teams_other_group: Heads up: user_impersonation is hidden inside a collapsed Other permissions group on the Delegated tab — expand that group to find the checkbox.
app_reg.perms_sp_missing: If one of the last three APIs doesn't appear even after pasting its App ID, its service principal isn't present in your tenant yet. Open Azure Cloud Shell (the >_ icon in the portal), run az ad sp create --id <App ID> for the missing API, then reopen Add a permission → APIs my organization uses and it will be there.
app_reg.consent_1: After adding all 58 permissions, click the Grant admin consent for <your tenant> button at the top of the API permissions table.
app_reg.consent_2: Confirm in the popup. Wait a few seconds for Microsoft to process.
app_reg.consent_3: Every permission row should now show a green checkmark in the Status column. If any are still amber/red, scroll down and click Grant admin consent again.
app_reg.ok_consent_check: When done, ALL 58 permissions should show green checkmarks. If any are missing, the Test Connection button on the next wizard step will tell you exactly which ones.
app_reg.h_roles: Assign Entra Roles to the App's Service Principal
app_reg.roles_intro: Panoptica365 uses PowerShell modules (Exchange Online + Compliance Center) for several security settings. These modules require Entra directory roles on top of the Graph permissions.
app_reg.roles_1: In the Entra portal sidebar, go to Identity → Roles & admins → Roles & administrators.
app_reg.roles_2: Search for Exchange Administrator. Click into the role.
app_reg.roles_3: Click Add assignments. Search for Panoptica365 (the name of your app reg) — it should appear as an enterprise application / service principal. Select it and click Add.
app_reg.roles_4: Repeat for Compliance Administrator: search the role list, add assignment, search Panoptica365, add.
app_reg.danger_role_names: Pick the EXACT role names — there are similar-sounding roles that will NOT work: Exchange Recipient Administrator (lesser scope), Compliance Data Administrator (lesser scope). Verify the name matches exactly Exchange Administrator and Compliance Administrator.
app_reg.groups_intro: Panoptica365 has three operator roles (admin / operator / viewer). Map each role to an Entra security group. Members of those groups get the corresponding role inside Panoptica365.
app_reg.groups_1: In the Entra portal sidebar, go to Identity → Groups → All groups → New group.
app_reg.groups_2: Group type: Security. Create three groups with these suggested names:
app_reg.group_admin_desc: full access to everything, including system + audit log
app_reg.group_operator_desc: day-to-day work — alerts, deploys, settings (no destructive ops)
app_reg.groups_naming_note: You can name them anything you want — the wizard will ask you for each group's Object ID, not its name.
app_reg.groups_3: For each group, after creation, copy the Object Id from the group's Overview page. You'll paste them into the next wizard step (admin is recommended; operator + viewer are optional).
app_reg.groups_4: Add yourself (and any colleagues) to the appropriate group(s).
app_reg.warn_skip_groups: If you skip group creation entirely and leave all three Object ID fields blank in the next step, any authenticated user from your MSP tenant gets full Admin access in Panoptica365. That's fine for single-operator installs — risky for multi-person MSPs.
app_reg.recap_intro: Before closing this modal and continuing the wizard, make sure you have all of these:
app_reg.recap_1: Directory (tenant) ID — GUID from the app's Overview page
app_reg.recap_2: Application (client) ID — GUID from the app's Overview page
app_reg.recap_3: Client Secret Value — the long random string from "Certificates & secrets" (NOT the Secret ID)
app_reg.recap_4: Admin group Object ID (recommended) — GUID from the "Panoptica365 Admins" group's Overview
app_reg.recap_5: Operator group Object ID (optional) — GUID from "Panoptica365 Operators"
app_reg.recap_6: Viewer group Object ID (optional) — GUID from "Panoptica365 Viewers"
app_reg.ok_close_modal: When you have those values, click I've completed the steps above at the bottom of this modal. The next wizard step is where you'll paste them, and a Test Connection button there will validate every permission was granted correctly.

entra.title: Entra Credentials
entra.subtitle: Paste the values you collected during the App Registration step.
entra.intro: Paste the Tenant ID, Client ID, and Secret Value you collected during the App Registration step. The Test Connection button at the bottom does a multi-permission spot-check to confirm everything was set up correctly.
entra.hint_secret: The secret value (not the secret ID). Visible only at creation time in the Entra portal.
entra.hint_admin_group: Members of this Entra group get the Admin role in Panoptica365. Leave blank to allow all authenticated users (single-operator setup).
entra.hint_member_group: Members get the Operator role — can clear alerts, deploy templates, generate reports. Leave blank to skip the Operator tier.
entra.hint_viewer_group: Members get the Viewer role — read-only access. Leave blank to skip the Viewer tier.
entra.reopen_modal_link: → Reopen the App Registration instructions modal
entra.test_ok: All representative permissions are granted. Credentials look correct.

smtp.title: Email (SMTP)
smtp.subtitle: Used for alert notifications + daily summary.
smtp.label_test_to: Send test email to (optional but recommended)
smtp.test_no_to: Enter a destination email address first.
smtp.test_ok: Test email sent to {to}. Check your inbox.

anthropic.title: Anthropic (Claude AI)
anthropic.subtitle: Powers alert analysis + daily summary + the Ask Claude widget.
anthropic.intro: Get an API key at console.anthropic.com. Estimated monthly cost across ~15 tenants: $5-15.
anthropic.test_ok: Anthropic API key works. Test call succeeded.

license.title: License Activation
license.subtitle: Activate this install against the Panoptica365 license server.
license.intro: Paste the 24-character activation key from your Panoptica365 license email (format: PNX-AAAA-BBBB-CCCC-DDDD-EEEE-FFFF or just the raw 24 chars). The wizard will exchange it for a license token and persist it to .env.

first_tenant.title: Add Your First Customer Tenant
first_tenant.subtitle: Optional — you can do this after setup completes.
first_tenant.intro: Panoptica365 monitors your customers' Microsoft 365 tenants. To start, you can grant admin consent to your first customer tenant now (you will be redirected to Microsoft, then back to the Panoptica365 dashboard), or skip this step and add tenants later from the Tenants page.
first_tenant.complete_note: When you click either button below, the wizard will mark setup complete. Subsequent boots will go straight to the dashboard.
